title: Film and video editors and camera operators
rationale: Film and video editing is almost entirely computer-based work involving organizing, cutting, and assembling digital footage — tasks where AI tools (automated editing, scene detection, color grading, audio syncing) are advancing rapidly and can already handle significant portions of the workflow. Camera operators have more physical, on-location work that creates a natural barrier to AI automation, though robotic cameras and drone technology are already reducing some roles. The split nature of this occupation — highly digital editing work combined with physical camera operation — lands it in the high exposure range, with editors facing greater displacement risk than operators.

title: Financial analysts
rationale: Financial analysts work almost entirely on computers, processing data, building models, writing reports, and making investment recommendations — all tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. AI tools can already automate much of the data gathering, financial statement analysis, trend identification, and report drafting that forms the core of this work. While client relationships, nuanced judgment, and accountability for recommendations provide some human-value buffer, the productivity gains from AI are likely to be so substantial that fewer analysts will be needed to produce the same output, making this occupation highly exposed to both automation and workforce reduction.

title: Financial clerks
rationale: Financial clerks perform almost entirely digital, rule-based tasks — record-keeping, transaction processing, billing, payroll, credit checking, and data entry — all of which are prime targets for AI and automation. The BLS already projects a 5% employment decline driven explicitly by productivity-enhancing technology and automated processing software, with some subcategories like payroll clerks declining 17%. While some roles involve customer interaction, the core work is structured information processing that AI can handle with high accuracy and speed.

title: Financial examiners
rationale: Financial examiners do predominantly knowledge work — reviewing documents, analyzing balance sheets, assessing risk, and writing compliance reports — all of which are tasks where AI can provide substantial assistance or automation. However, the role also involves significant regulatory judgment, institutional relationships, onsite inspections, and nuanced interpretation of complex situations that still require human expertise and accountability. The strong projected job growth (19%) suggests regulatory demand is rising, but AI tools will likely make individual examiners substantially more productive, potentially moderating long-term headcount growth.

title: Financial managers
rationale: Financial managers perform predominantly knowledge-based work — analyzing data, preparing reports, forecasting, and advising executives — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and can meaningfully augment or partially automate tasks. However, the role also involves significant human judgment, stakeholder relationships, regulatory expertise, and leadership responsibilities that are harder to automate. AI will likely make individual financial managers substantially more productive (automating report generation, data analysis, and compliance monitoring), potentially reducing headcount needs even as demand for the function grows.

title: Fire inspectors
rationale: Fire inspection and investigation is fundamentally a physical, on-site occupation requiring in-person building visits, debris analysis, evidence collection, and field patrols in unpredictable environments. AI can assist with documentation, report writing, code compliance checking, and records management, but cannot replace the physical presence required to inspect buildings, assess fire scenes, or patrol forests. The core value of the job lies in hands-on observation, physical evidence gathering, and real-world judgment that AI cannot replicate remotely.

title: Firefighters
rationale: Firefighting is overwhelmingly a physical, hands-on occupation requiring real-time presence in dangerous, unpredictable environments — tasks AI cannot perform. AI might assist with peripheral tasks like incident report writing, scheduling, or predictive fire risk modeling, but these represent a tiny fraction of the job. The core duties of extinguishing fires, rescuing victims, and providing emergency medical care are fundamentally resistant to AI automation.

title: Fishing and hunting workers
rationale: Fishing and hunting work is almost entirely physical, hands-on, and conducted in unpredictable outdoor and marine environments. Core tasks — hauling nets, operating vessels, setting traps, sorting catches — require physical presence and manual skill that AI cannot replicate. While AI-assisted fish-finding sonar, GPS navigation, and quota management software exist, these are peripheral tools that don't fundamentally restructure the labor-intensive nature of the work.

title: Fitness trainers and instructors
rationale: Fitness training is fundamentally a physical, in-person occupation centered on demonstrating exercises, observing client form, providing real-time corrections, and delivering motivation through human presence and relationship. AI can assist with peripheral tasks like program design, scheduling, or content creation for social media, but the core value proposition — a human trainer physically present to guide, correct, and motivate — is difficult to automate. While AI-powered fitness apps and virtual trainers exist as lower-cost alternatives, the strong projected job growth suggests the human element remains highly valued and not easily substituted.

title: Flight attendants
rationale: Flight attendants perform primarily physical, in-person work aboard aircraft — serving passengers, conducting safety demonstrations, managing emergencies, and providing hands-on care — that cannot be done remotely or digitally. Federal regulations mandate minimum staffing levels, and the core safety and service functions require human presence and judgment in real-time, unpredictable environments. AI may assist with minor administrative tasks like report writing or scheduling, but it cannot meaningfully automate the central duties of this role.

title: Flooring installers and tile and stone setters
rationale: Flooring installation and tile/stone setting is almost entirely physical, hands-on work requiring workers to be present on-site, kneeling, cutting, measuring, and placing materials in highly variable environments. AI has essentially no ability to automate the core tasks of this job. Minor peripheral benefits might come from AI-assisted design planning or material estimation, but these are a negligible fraction of the actual work.

title: Floral designers
rationale: Floral design is fundamentally a hands-on, physical craft requiring manual dexterity, tactile judgment about flower freshness, and in-person customer interaction — all areas where AI has limited direct impact. AI could assist with design inspiration, order management, or inventory systems, but the core work of physically arranging flowers, setting up event displays, and advising customers in real time remains human-dependent. The occupation is already projected to decline due to consolidation and online ordering platforms rather than AI specifically.

title: Food and beverage serving and related workers
rationale: Food and beverage serving work is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — taking orders, carrying food, cleaning tables, and interacting face-to-face with customers in real time. While AI and automation (kiosks, ordering apps) can handle some peripheral tasks like order-taking and payment processing, the core physical presence, manual labor, and human service elements create strong barriers to AI displacement. The occupation's projected growth of 5% further reflects its resilience to automation.

title: Food preparation workers
rationale: Food preparation work is almost entirely physical and hands-on, requiring manual dexterity, physical stamina, and real-time presence in a kitchen environment. AI has essentially no direct role in chopping vegetables, cutting meat, or cleaning equipment, though automation (robotics, pre-processed ingredients from suppliers) poses some indirect displacement risk. The projected employment decline is driven more by supply chain efficiencies and labor substitution than by AI specifically.

title: Food processing equipment workers
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to operate, monitor, load, and clean large industrial food processing machinery in a factory environment. While AI-driven automation could incrementally improve process monitoring or quality control, the core tasks involve physical presence, manual operation, and sensory judgment that are difficult to replace with AI software. The minor data recording and recipe-following components have some digital exposure, but these are peripheral to the job's physical demands.

title: Food service managers
rationale: Food service managers work primarily in physical environments requiring constant on-site presence — overseeing kitchen operations, inspecting facilities, managing staff in real time, and handling customer issues face-to-face. While some administrative tasks like scheduling, budgeting, payroll, and recordkeeping could be assisted by AI tools, these represent a minority of the role's demands. The core job is fundamentally tied to physical presence, interpersonal leadership, and hands-on operational management in an unpredictable environment.

title: Forensic science technicians
rationale: Forensic science technicians have a hybrid role combining physical crime scene work (collecting evidence, photographing scenes) with laboratory analysis and report writing. AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and reporting components — DNA matching, database comparisons, pattern recognition, and report generation — but the physical evidence collection, chain-of-custody handling, and courtroom testimony require human presence and judgment. The job's strong projected growth suggests demand is rising faster than AI can displace it, though AI tools will likely make lab analysis more efficient over time.

title: Forest and conservation workers
rationale: Forest and conservation workers perform almost entirely physical, outdoor tasks — planting seedlings, clearing brush, cutting trees, suppressing fires, and maintaining equipment — that require hands-on presence in unpredictable natural environments. While AI and remote sensing technology (e.g., drone-based tree counting) can automate some peripheral data-collection tasks, the core manual labor is highly resistant to AI substitution. The BLS itself attributes projected employment decline primarily to mechanical automation and remote sensing rather than AI, and the job has essentially no digital knowledge-work component.

title: Fundraisers
rationale: Fundraising involves significant knowledge work — researching donors, crafting messaging, managing campaigns, maintaining records, and using digital platforms — all areas where AI can provide substantial assistance. However, the core of the job relies heavily on relationship-building, interpersonal trust, and in-person engagement with donors, which creates a meaningful barrier to full AI substitution. AI can automate donor research, personalize outreach, and optimize campaign strategies, making individual fundraisers more productive, but the human relationship element remains central to securing major gifts and planned giving.

title: Funeral service workers
rationale: Funeral service work is heavily grounded in physical tasks (body preparation, embalming, transportation of remains) and high-stakes interpersonal interactions with grieving families that require genuine human empathy and presence. AI could assist with administrative tasks like filing death certificates, drafting obituaries, or managing scheduling, but these peripheral tasks represent a small fraction of the job's core value. The deeply human nature of grief counseling and the physical requirements of body preparation create strong barriers to meaningful AI displacement.

title: Gambling services workers
rationale: Gambling services work is fundamentally a physical, in-person occupation requiring real-time human interaction on a casino floor — dealing cards, monitoring players, managing staff, and ensuring customer experience. While online gambling and automated betting systems are already displacing some roles (particularly sports book writers and runners), the core dealer and supervisor roles depend on physical presence and interpersonal engagement that AI cannot easily replicate. AI may assist with back-office tasks like scheduling or fraud detection, but it doesn't substantially reshape the daily work of most gambling services workers.

title: General maintenance and repair workers
rationale: General maintenance and repair work is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to be physically present to diagnose and fix mechanical, electrical, plumbing, and structural problems in buildings and equipment. AI might assist with scheduling, work order management, or diagnostic guidance, but the core tasks — using hand tools, climbing ladders, replacing components, and troubleshooting physical systems — cannot be automated by AI. The occupation's high injury rate and reliance on dexterity, physical presence, and real-world problem-solving create strong natural barriers to AI displacement.

title: General office clerks
rationale: General office clerks perform almost entirely digital and information-processing tasks — data entry, document preparation, filing, answering phones, scheduling — all of which are core AI automation targets. The BLS itself projects a 7% employment decline driven by automation of clerical tasks, and AI tools like intelligent document processing, automated scheduling, and virtual assistants directly replace the core duties of this role. While some human presence for customer interaction and physical mail handling remains, the vast majority of the work is routine, digital, and highly susceptible to AI-driven automation.

title: Genetic counselors
rationale: Genetic counselors do substantial knowledge work — analyzing genetic data, interpreting test results, researching hereditary conditions, and documenting records — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate. However, a core and irreplaceable component of the role involves emotionally sensitive counseling sessions with distressed patients and families, requiring empathy, interpersonal skills, and nuanced human judgment that AI cannot replicate. AI tools will likely make individual counselors more productive (faster literature review, automated risk scoring, documentation assistance) but the human-facing counseling dimension provides a meaningful buffer against full displacement.

title: Geographers
rationale: Geographers do predominantly knowledge-based work — analyzing spatial data, creating maps, writing reports, and interpreting GIS datasets — all of which are highly amenable to AI augmentation. AI tools are already transforming GIS analysis, satellite imagery interpretation, and spatial pattern recognition, meaning fewer geographers may be needed to produce the same outputs. Some fieldwork and qualitative research (interviews, field observations) provides a partial buffer, but the core analytical and visualization tasks are deeply digital and increasingly automatable.

title: Geological and hydrologic technicians
rationale: This occupation is split between physical fieldwork (collecting samples, installing equipment in remote locations, physical stamina requirements) and office/lab work (data analysis, report writing, GIS mapping). AI can meaningfully assist with the data analysis, report generation, and mapping components, but the core fieldwork — operating equipment in the field, collecting physical samples, and maintaining instruments — requires human presence and cannot be automated easily. The BLS itself notes that drones and technology may reduce demand for some geological technicians, suggesting moderate but not transformative AI/automation exposure.

title: Geoscientists
rationale: Geoscientists split their time between fieldwork (collecting samples, conducting surveys in remote locations), laboratory analysis, and office-based data analysis and report writing. AI can significantly assist with the knowledge-work components — analyzing geological data, processing remote sensing imagery, running GIS models, and drafting reports — but the substantial fieldwork, physical sample collection, and on-site judgment remain barriers to full AI substitution. The combination of physical presence requirements and specialized scientific interpretation keeps exposure moderate rather than high.

title: Glaziers
rationale: Glaziers perform highly physical, hands-on work involving cutting, lifting, and installing glass at job sites, which cannot be done remotely or digitally. AI might assist marginally with blueprint reading, measurements, or scheduling, but the core tasks require physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time judgment in variable environments. There is essentially no pathway for AI to automate the primary work of this occupation.

title: Graphic designers
rationale: Graphic design is almost entirely computer-based work involving visual creation, layout, and digital communication — all domains where AI (image generation, automated design tools, layout AI) is advancing rapidly. The BLS itself notes that AI tools may reduce demand for freelance graphic designers, and the job outlook is already sluggish at 2% growth. While human creativity, client relationship management, and brand strategy judgment still add value, the core production tasks of generating visuals, logos, layouts, and illustrations are increasingly automatable, placing this occupation firmly in the very high exposure range.

title: Grounds maintenance workers
rationale: Grounds maintenance work is almost entirely physical and outdoors, involving mowing, planting, trimming, and applying treatments — tasks that require hands-on manual labor in unpredictable natural environments. AI has essentially no direct impact on the core work, though it might marginally assist with scheduling, route optimization, or pest diagnosis. Autonomous robotic mowers exist but remain limited in scope, and the diversity of tasks (tree trimming, hardscaping, chemical application) presents significant barriers to full automation.

title: Hand laborers and material movers
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical — lifting, moving, packing, cleaning, and collecting materials in real-world environments — which creates a natural barrier to direct AI automation. However, robotics and warehouse automation (conveyor systems, autonomous mobile robots, self-checkout) are gradually displacing some roles like machine feeders and hand packers, representing an indirect AI/automation effect. The core work still requires human physical presence and dexterity in variable environments, keeping overall AI exposure relatively low, though not negligible.

title: Hazardous materials removal workers
rationale: Hazardous materials removal is fundamentally a physical, hands-on occupation requiring workers to be present at contaminated sites, operate heavy equipment, wear protective gear, and manually remove dangerous substances. AI has essentially no ability to perform the core tasks of scraping asbestos, sandblasting lead paint, or decontaminating nuclear facilities. The only peripheral tasks AI might assist with are record-keeping, regulatory compliance documentation, or site planning, but these represent a small fraction of the job.

title: Health and safety engineers
rationale: Health and safety engineers split their time between office-based knowledge work (reviewing plans, writing safety programs, analyzing regulations, preparing reports) and physical site inspections, accident investigations, and interpersonal interactions with workers. AI can meaningfully assist with the digital components—regulatory research, documentation, risk analysis, and report generation—but the core job requires physical presence at worksites, observational judgment in real environments, and trust-building interpersonal skills that AI cannot replicate. The occupation's reliance on site visits, hands-on hazard identification, and human interviews creates a natural barrier that keeps AI exposure at a moderate level.

title: Health education specialists
rationale: Health education specialists do substantial knowledge work — developing materials, analyzing data, writing grant proposals, and designing programs — that AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, a significant portion of the role involves interpersonal engagement: working one-on-one with patients, facilitating community programs, building trust with diverse populations, and advocating with policymakers, all of which require human presence and empathy. AI tools can boost productivity in the information-processing and content-creation dimensions, but the community-facing, relationship-driven core of the job provides a meaningful buffer against full automation.

title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely computer-based, involving organizing clinical databases, analyzing healthcare data, generating reports, and managing electronic health records — all tasks where AI can provide substantial assistance or automation. AI tools are already being deployed for data abstraction, coding, and registry management, which are core duties of medical registrars. However, the role also requires domain expertise, regulatory compliance judgment, collaboration with clinical staff, and system implementation advisory work that still demands human oversight, keeping it from the highest exposure tier.

title: Heating, air conditioning, and refrigeration mechanics and installers
rationale: HVAC work is fundamentally physical and hands-on — installing, repairing, and maintaining climate-control systems in buildings requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-world problem-solving that AI cannot replicate. AI might assist with diagnostics, scheduling, or record-keeping at the margins, but the core job tasks are deeply resistant to automation. The occupation is also projected to grow strongly, reflecting robust demand that AI is unlikely to suppress.

title: Heavy and tractor-trailer truck drivers
rationale: Truck driving is fundamentally a physical, real-world task requiring manual skill, situational awareness, and presence in unpredictable environments, creating a natural barrier to AI automation. However, autonomous vehicle technology (self-driving trucks) represents a significant long-term threat, and AI already assists with route optimization, logistics management, and electronic logging. The BLS still projects modest growth through 2034, suggesting full automation remains distant, but the trajectory of autonomous trucking technology keeps exposure above the purely manual-labor baseline.

title: Heavy vehicle and mobile equipment service technicians
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally hands-on and physical — disassembling engines, replacing hydraulic components, welding, and working in awkward positions on heavy machinery. AI can assist with diagnostics (reading sensor data, suggesting fault codes) and documentation, but the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and mechanical skill that AI cannot replicate. The job outlook is actually growing faster than average, reflecting strong demand for human technicians despite increasing equipment computerization.

title: High school teachers
rationale: High school teaching involves a significant mix of physical presence, interpersonal relationship-building, and classroom management that AI cannot easily replicate, alongside knowledge-work tasks like lesson planning, grading, and content delivery where AI can provide substantial assistance. AI tools can already automate grading, generate lesson plans, personalize learning materials, and explain concepts, potentially making each teacher more productive or reducing demand at the margins. However, the core value of teaching — motivating students, managing classroom dynamics, mentoring, and adapting in real-time to human behavior — remains deeply human and requires physical presence, limiting how far AI can restructure the role.

title: Historians
rationale: Historians do predominantly knowledge work — researching, analyzing, interpreting, and writing about historical sources — which is highly amenable to AI assistance in literature review, synthesis, translation, and drafting. However, the job also involves physical archival work, artifact handling, fieldwork, public engagement, and nuanced interpretive judgment that requires deep domain expertise and contextual understanding. AI can significantly accelerate research and writing tasks, but the scholarly judgment, source authentication, and public-facing roles retain meaningful human requirements.

title: Home health and personal care aides
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — bathing, dressing, lifting, cooking, and providing companionship to clients in their homes. AI cannot replicate the hands-on caregiving, physical presence, or emotional support that defines this role. AI might marginally assist with scheduling, record-keeping, or care plan documentation, but these are peripheral to the core job, which is projected to grow 17% as demand for in-person care rises.

title: Human resources managers
rationale: HR managers do predominantly knowledge-based work — writing policies, analyzing workforce data, managing compliance, and communicating — all areas where AI can provide substantial assistance. However, the role is heavily interpersonal, involving dispute mediation, employee relations, executive consultation, and leadership of teams, which require human judgment and trust that AI cannot fully replicate. AI will likely automate routine HR tasks (payroll processing, benefits administration, initial screening) and boost individual productivity, but the strategic advisory and relationship-management core of the role provides meaningful insulation from full displacement.

title: Human resources specialists
rationale: HR specialists do predominantly knowledge-based, office work involving recruiting, screening, record-keeping, and compliance — all areas where AI tools are already making workers significantly more productive (e.g., resume screening, job description writing, background check automation, policy Q&A). However, a meaningful portion of the role involves interpersonal judgment — interviewing candidates, resolving employee disputes, building relationships — which still requires human presence and nuanced social skills. The net effect is high but not transformative exposure: AI will reshape workflows and reduce headcount needs at the margins, but the human-facing and judgment-intensive core of the role provides a meaningful buffer.

title: Hydrologists
rationale: Hydrology involves significant knowledge work — data analysis, modeling, report writing, and forecasting — that AI can meaningfully assist with, particularly in processing large datasets, running predictive models, and automating routine analysis. However, a substantial portion of the job requires physical fieldwork: collecting water and soil samples, measuring streamflow, and traveling to remote locations, which AI cannot replace. The BLS itself notes that 'integrated technology and review systems may limit the need for some hydrologists,' suggesting AI-driven tools are already beginning to reshape the occupation, but the irreducible field component keeps exposure from reaching the highest tiers.

title: Industrial designers
rationale: Industrial design is predominantly knowledge and creative work done on computers — sketching, CAD modeling, rendering, client communication — all domains where AI is rapidly advancing and already useful (generative design, AI-assisted CAD, image generation). However, the job retains meaningful physical and interpersonal components: creating physical prototypes, traveling to manufacturing sites, collaborating with engineers, and consulting with clients, which provide some natural barriers to full AI displacement. AI tools will likely make individual designers substantially more productive (faster ideation, automated rendering, generative design exploration), potentially reducing headcount needed, placing this firmly in the high-exposure range.

title: Industrial engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This role combines significant knowledge work (data analysis, workflow design, statistical studies, preparing charts and diagrams) with substantial physical presence requirements — technicians must work on-site at manufacturing facilities to observe operations, conduct time-motion studies, and assess machinery. AI can meaningfully assist with data collection analysis, process optimization modeling, and documentation, but the core observational and hands-on assessment tasks require physical presence in production environments. The role's slow projected growth and emphasis on automation assistance suggests AI will augment rather than eliminate these workers in the near term.

title: Industrial engineers
rationale: Industrial engineers do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, process optimization, system design, and reporting — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, making individual engineers significantly more productive. However, a meaningful portion of the job involves physical site visits, observing workers and processes firsthand, collaborating across departments, and implementing changes in real-world environments, which creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement. The occupation's projected growth suggests AI is more likely to augment these engineers (enabling fewer to do more) than to eliminate the role outright, but productivity gains could moderate headcount growth over time.

title: Industrial machinery mechanics, machinery maintenance workers, and millwrights
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally hands-on and physical — mechanics and millwrights must be physically present to disassemble, repair, calibrate, and install industrial machinery. AI may assist with predictive maintenance scheduling, diagnostic analysis, and reading technical manuals, but the core work of physically fixing and installing complex machinery cannot be automated by AI. The strong projected job growth (13%) driven by increased automation in manufacturing actually suggests complementarity with technology rather than displacement.

title: Industrial production managers
rationale: Industrial production managers split their time between office-based knowledge work (analyzing production data, reviewing reports, budgeting, scheduling) and physical plant oversight requiring on-site presence, safety compliance, and real-time human leadership. AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and planning tasks — optimizing schedules, flagging quality issues, forecasting supply needs — but the core job involves managing people, navigating physical production environments, and making judgment calls that require contextual, on-the-ground awareness. The strong interpersonal, leadership, and problem-solving dimensions tied to physical operations create a natural ceiling on AI displacement.

title: Information clerks
rationale: Information clerks perform largely routine, digital tasks — maintaining records, processing data, answering standard inquiries, and preparing reports — that are highly amenable to AI automation through chatbots, automated form processing, and intelligent document management systems. The BLS already projects employment decline driven by self-service systems and electronic data collection, confirming AI and automation are actively displacing these roles. Some sub-occupations (hotel desk clerks, eligibility interviewers) retain meaningful face-to-face human interaction that provides partial insulation, keeping the score from reaching the highest tier.

title: Information security analysts
rationale: Information security analysis is predominantly knowledge work done on computers — monitoring networks, analyzing threats, writing reports, and developing security policies — making it highly amenable to AI augmentation. AI tools are already being deployed for threat detection, vulnerability scanning, and log analysis, meaning each analyst can handle more with AI assistance, potentially reducing headcount needs over time. However, the adversarial, rapidly evolving nature of cybersecurity requires continuous human judgment, creative problem-solving, and organizational communication that keeps this from reaching the highest exposure tier, and ironically AI-driven threats are simultaneously increasing demand for these workers.

title: Instructional coordinators
rationale: Instructional coordinators do substantial knowledge work — analyzing student data, developing curricula, reviewing educational materials, and writing training content — all of which AI can meaningfully assist with or accelerate. However, a significant portion of the role involves interpersonal and leadership activities: observing teachers in classrooms, conducting workshops, mentoring staff, and building relationships with administrators, which require physical presence and human judgment. AI tools could make individual coordinators substantially more productive (e.g., faster curriculum analysis, automated data review), potentially reducing headcount, but the relational and institutional dimensions of the job create a meaningful barrier to full automation.

title: Insulation workers
rationale: Insulation work is fundamentally a hands-on, physical trade requiring workers to measure, cut, fit, and install materials in confined spaces, on scaffolding, and around complex mechanical systems — tasks that cannot be performed remotely or digitally. AI might marginally assist with blueprint analysis or material quantity estimation, but the core job involves physical dexterity, strength, and on-site judgment that AI cannot replicate. This occupation has very low AI exposure and is far more constrained by physical labor availability than by information processing.

title: Insurance sales agents
rationale: Insurance sales is primarily a knowledge and communication-based role involving explaining policies, analyzing client needs, and maintaining relationships — all areas where AI can provide significant assistance through lead generation, policy comparison, document drafting, and customer interaction tools. However, the job retains a strong interpersonal and trust-building component that is difficult to fully automate, as clients often rely on human agents for guidance on complex, high-stakes financial decisions. AI is already reducing demand at the margins (online self-service purchasing), but the consultative, relationship-driven core of the role provides a meaningful buffer against full automation.

title: Insurance underwriters
rationale: Insurance underwriting is almost entirely knowledge work done at a computer — analyzing data, evaluating risk factors, and making coverage decisions — all of which are core AI strengths. The BLS itself notes that automated underwriting software is already reducing the need for human underwriters, with employment projected to decline, and AI/ML models are increasingly capable of handling even complex risk assessments that previously required human judgment. The only partial barriers are edge cases requiring nuanced human judgment, regulatory accountability, and occasional physical property inspections, but these represent a small fraction of the work.

title: Interior designers
rationale: Interior design involves substantial digital work — CAD/BIM software, space planning, material specification, cost estimation, and client presentations — all areas where AI tools are rapidly advancing and already useful. However, the job also requires significant physical presence (site visits, client relationship management, overseeing installations) and creative judgment that is deeply contextual and interpersonal. AI can accelerate visualization, mood boarding, and specification tasks considerably, but the blend of physical site work, client collaboration, and regulatory navigation keeps this from being a purely digital role.

title: Interpreters and translators
rationale: Translation is fundamentally a digital, text-based task where AI (especially large language models) has already achieved near-human quality for many language pairs, and machine translation post-editing is already standard practice in the industry. Written translation faces the highest displacement risk, as AI tools can handle large volumes rapidly with human review. Interpreting has more resilience due to real-time human presence requirements, cultural nuance, and specialized domains (legal, medical, sign language), but even here remote AI-assisted interpreting is advancing quickly, explaining the already-slow projected job growth.

title: Ironworkers
rationale: Ironworking is fundamentally a physical, hands-on trade requiring workers to lift, cut, bend, weld, and connect heavy steel and iron components at construction sites, often at great heights and in variable conditions. AI has essentially no ability to perform these core tasks, which demand physical strength, balance, depth perception, and real-time adaptation to unpredictable environments. Minor peripheral tasks like blueprint reading or scheduling could see some AI assistance, but the vast majority of the job remains firmly in the physical domain.

title: Janitors and building cleaners
rationale: Janitorial work is almost entirely physical and hands-on — sweeping, mopping, cleaning restrooms, lifting supplies, making minor repairs — tasks that require physical presence and cannot be done remotely or digitally. While robotic cleaning equipment (autonomous floor scrubbers, etc.) exists and may gradually displace some workers, this is more automation than AI per se, and the unpredictable, varied physical environments still require human adaptability. AI has essentially no impact on the core daily tasks of this occupation.

title: Jewelers and precious stone and metal workers
rationale: Jewelry work is fundamentally a hands-on, manual craft requiring fine motor dexterity, physical tools, and tactile skill to shape metals, set stones, and repair pieces — tasks AI cannot directly perform. While AI and CAD/CAM tools can assist with design and 3D printing is already automating some production steps (contributing to the projected employment decline), the core bench work remains physical and artisanal. The gemology and appraisal aspects have some knowledge-work component that AI could assist with, but the dominant share of the occupation is physical craftsmanship.

title: Judges and hearing officers
rationale: Judges and hearing officers perform substantial knowledge work — researching legal issues, reading briefs, writing opinions, and applying precedent — that AI can meaningfully assist with, pushing exposure upward. However, the role is fundamentally anchored in physical courtroom presence, human judgment on credibility and fairness, constitutional authority, and public trust that society is unlikely to delegate to AI systems. The institutional, political, and ethical barriers to AI replacing judicial decision-making are exceptionally high, even as AI tools could significantly augment research and drafting tasks.

title: Kindergarten and elementary school teachers
rationale: Elementary teaching involves significant physical presence, real-time human interaction, and relationship-building with young children that AI cannot replicate — supervising recess, managing classroom behavior, and nurturing social-emotional development are inherently human tasks. However, AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, grading, generating instructional materials, and personalizing learning content, making teachers more productive in their knowledge-work tasks. The core of the job remains anchored in physical presence and interpersonal care for children, limiting AI's ability to fully restructure the occupation even as it becomes a useful tool.

title: Labor relations specialists
rationale: Labor relations specialists do substantial knowledge work — drafting contracts, analyzing labor law, writing policies, researching grievances — that AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, the core of the job involves high-stakes interpersonal negotiation, dispute resolution, and building trust between adversarial parties, which requires human judgment and relationship skills that AI cannot replicate. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level where AI tools will boost individual productivity (e.g., contract drafting, compliance research) but the irreducibly human elements of mediation and negotiation provide a meaningful buffer.

title: Landscape architects
rationale: Landscape architecture is a mix of digital knowledge work (CADD design, GIS analysis, cost estimation, client presentations, environmental analysis) and physical site work (inspections, material selection, on-site coordination). AI tools can significantly assist with the design, visualization, and analytical components — generative design, automated drafting, environmental modeling — potentially making individual architects much more productive. However, the job retains meaningful physical presence requirements, creative judgment about natural environments, and client relationship management that limit full automation.

title: Lawyers
rationale: Legal work is predominantly knowledge-based — research, document drafting, contract review, and legal analysis — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and already demonstrating strong capability. However, core lawyering involves significant human elements: courtroom advocacy, client relationships, negotiation, and the exercise of judgment in high-stakes, nuanced situations that still require human presence and accountability. The BLS itself notes that 'some routine legal work may be automated,' and AI tools like contract review, legal research, and document generation are already reshaping the profession, but the irreducibly interpersonal and adversarial nature of legal practice provides a meaningful buffer against full automation.

title: Librarians and library media specialists
rationale: Librarians do substantial knowledge work — research assistance, database management, collection curation, and information organization — that AI can increasingly handle, and AI-powered search tools directly threaten the core reference and research-assistance functions. However, significant portions of the role involve physical presence (managing physical collections, running community programs, working with patrons face-to-face, supervising staff), and the job has strong institutional and community-service dimensions that resist full automation. The slow projected job growth suggests AI and digital resources are already suppressing demand, but the interpersonal, educational, and community-programming aspects provide meaningful insulation.

title: Library technicians and assistants
rationale: Library technicians and assistants perform a mix of physical tasks (reshelving books, managing physical materials) and information-processing tasks (cataloguing, answering reference questions, maintaining databases) that AI can increasingly handle. AI-powered search tools and digital catalogs already reduce the need for human assistance in locating information, and the occupation is already projected to decline 7%. However, significant physical presence, patron interaction, and community programming roles provide some buffer against full automation.

title: Licensed practical and licensed vocational nurses
rationale: LPN/LVN work is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — monitoring vitals, changing bandages, bathing patients, administering medications, and providing hands-on care that requires physical presence in healthcare settings. AI can assist with documentation, health record management, and some diagnostic support, but these are peripheral to the core job. The high injury rate and physical stamina requirements underscore that this is an embodied, in-person occupation with strong natural barriers to AI automation.

title: Loan officers
rationale: Loan officers perform predominantly knowledge-based work — gathering financial data, running credit analyses, and making approval decisions — much of which is already being automated through underwriting software. AI can further automate credit evaluation, document verification, risk scoring, and compliance checks, reducing the number of officers needed per loan processed. However, the sales, relationship-building, and client-guidance components (especially for complex commercial and mortgage loans) provide some buffer, keeping this from the highest exposure tier.

title: Lodging managers
rationale: Lodging managers split their time between physical presence tasks (inspecting rooms, greeting guests, resolving on-site problems, supervising staff) and knowledge work (budgeting, scheduling, revenue management, communications). AI can meaningfully assist with revenue optimization, scheduling, customer communications, and administrative tasks, but the core job requires physical presence, real-time interpersonal judgment, and hands-on facility oversight. The occupation is unlikely to be heavily restructured by AI, though productivity tools may reduce the need for some administrative support roles around it.

title: Logging workers
rationale: Logging is overwhelmingly physical, outdoor work involving operating heavy machinery, felling trees, and handling logs in unpredictable natural environments — all of which are highly resistant to AI automation. The log grading/scaling sub-role has some data entry and estimation components where AI could assist marginally, but this is a small fraction of the workforce. AI has essentially no impact on the core daily tasks of cutting, moving, and processing timber in the field.

title: Logisticians
rationale: Logisticians do predominantly knowledge work — analyzing supply chains, forecasting demand, coordinating procurement, and managing data systems — all of which are areas where AI tools can meaningfully augment productivity. However, the role also involves significant interpersonal coordination with suppliers, customers, and internal teams, as well as real-time problem-solving in physical supply chain contexts that require human judgment and relationships. AI can automate routine optimization, demand forecasting, and data analysis tasks, but the liaison, negotiation, and adaptive problem-solving dimensions provide some buffer against full automation.

title: Machinists and tool and die makers
rationale: Machinists and tool and die makers work primarily in physical manufacturing environments requiring hands-on operation of machinery, manual dexterity, and real-time physical judgment — all natural barriers to AI displacement. However, a meaningful portion of their work involves digital tasks like CAD/CAM programming, CNC code generation, and blueprint interpretation, where AI tools are increasingly capable and already contributing to efficiency gains. The projected employment decline, particularly for tool and die makers (-11%), reflects automation's growing role, but the irreducible physical presence and tactile skill requirements keep overall AI exposure moderate rather than high.

title: Management analysts
rationale: Management analysts do predominantly knowledge work — gathering data, analyzing financials, developing recommendations, and writing reports — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and already providing substantial productivity gains. However, a significant portion of the job involves client-facing interpersonal work: interviewing personnel, conducting onsite observations, building trust with managers, and navigating organizational politics, which creates a meaningful barrier to full AI substitution. AI will likely make individual analysts far more productive (accelerating data analysis, report writing, and proposal generation), potentially reducing headcount needed per engagement, but the relationship-driven and judgment-intensive consulting role will persist in some form.

title: Manicurists and pedicurists
rationale: Manicurists and pedicurists perform almost entirely hands-on, physical work requiring manual dexterity, direct client contact, and fine motor skills that AI cannot replicate. The core tasks — filing, trimming, polishing, massaging, and applying nail art — require physical presence and tactile skill with no meaningful digital component. AI might marginally assist with scheduling or business administration for salon owners, but this represents a negligible fraction of the job.

title: Marine engineers and naval architects
rationale: Marine engineering and naval architecture is predominantly knowledge work involving design, analysis, simulation, and technical documentation — all areas where AI tools (CAD automation, generative design, fluid dynamics simulation, structural analysis) are advancing rapidly and can meaningfully augment productivity. However, the job also involves significant physical oversight of prototypes, testing, on-site inspections, regulatory coordination, and sometimes working at sea, which creates natural barriers to full AI substitution. The highly specialized, safety-critical, and regulatory nature of the work means human judgment and professional licensure remain essential, limiting exposure compared to purely digital knowledge work.

title: Market research analysts
rationale: Market research analysts work is almost entirely digital — collecting, analyzing, and synthesizing data, writing reports, creating visualizations, and presenting findings — all tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. AI tools can already automate significant portions of survey design, data analysis, trend forecasting, and report generation, potentially allowing far fewer analysts to produce the same output. While human judgment is still needed for strategic interpretation and client communication, the core analytical and reporting tasks are highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation.

title: Marriage and family therapists
rationale: Marriage and family therapy is fundamentally built on human relationships, empathy, and therapeutic rapport — qualities that AI cannot replicate in a clinical setting. While AI can assist with administrative tasks like record-keeping, scheduling, and insurance documentation, the core work of conducting therapy sessions, building trust with clients, and making nuanced clinical judgments requires irreplaceable human presence and interpersonal skill. The growing demand for mental health services and the deeply relational nature of the work provide strong structural barriers to AI displacement.

title: Masonry workers
rationale: Masonry work is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to lift heavy materials, mix and apply mortar, cut stone, and build structures in outdoor environments — tasks that AI cannot perform. AI might marginally assist with blueprint reading, materials estimation, or scheduling, but these are peripheral to the core job. The work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time adaptation to site conditions, creating a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

title: Massage therapists
rationale: Massage therapy is fundamentally a hands-on, physical occupation where the core work product — manipulating soft tissues and joints on a human body — cannot be digitized or automated by AI. The job requires physical presence, manual dexterity, strength, and real-time tactile feedback that AI has no pathway to replicate. AI might marginally assist with scheduling, client notes, or business administration tasks, but these are peripheral to the core work.

title: Material moving machine operators
rationale: Material moving machine operators perform primarily physical, hands-on work requiring real-time spatial judgment, manual control of heavy equipment, and on-site presence — all natural barriers to AI automation. However, autonomous forklifts, automated guided vehicles (AGVs), and warehouse robotics are already displacing some roles (particularly industrial truck operators), and AI-driven logistics optimization reduces the number of operators needed per facility. The BLS itself notes that automated machinery and sensors are limiting employment growth, making this a moderate-exposure occupation where AI's indirect effects (fewer workers needed due to automation) are more significant than direct task substitution.

title: Material recording clerks
rationale: Material recording clerks perform largely information-processing tasks — tracking inventory, recordkeeping, compiling reports, and managing schedules — that are highly amenable to automation through AI, RFID systems, and warehouse management software. The occupation already shows a projected 6% employment decline driven by automation technologies, confirming real displacement pressure. While some physical components exist (handling packages, inspecting goods on warehouse floors), the core value-add is data tracking and record management, which AI and automated systems can increasingly handle end-to-end.

title: Materials engineers
rationale: Materials engineers do substantial knowledge work — modeling, simulation, report writing, data analysis, and material property prediction — all areas where AI is increasingly capable and already being applied (e.g., machine learning for materials discovery). However, a significant portion of the job involves laboratory work, physical testing, factory environments, and hands-on problem-solving that requires physical presence and real-world experimentation. AI will likely accelerate materials discovery and reduce some analytical workload, but the combination of physical lab work, specialized domain expertise, and collaborative engineering judgment limits exposure compared to purely digital occupations.

title: Mathematicians and statisticians
rationale: Mathematicians and statisticians work almost entirely in digital domains — analyzing data, building models, writing code, and communicating findings — all areas where AI is rapidly advancing and already providing substantial productivity gains. AI tools can automate routine statistical analysis, generate code, identify patterns in large datasets, and assist with model building, meaning fewer specialists may be needed for standard tasks. However, the occupation retains meaningful human judgment requirements in problem framing, experimental design, interpreting results in context, and communicating nuanced findings to stakeholders, which tempers the exposure somewhat below the highest tier.

title: Mechanical engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This role combines significant physical/hands-on work (setting up tests, assembling mechanical parts, operating machinery, troubleshooting equipment on factory floors) with knowledge work (CAD design, data analysis, calculations, reviewing blueprints). AI can meaningfully automate the design, drafting, and analytical tasks — and the BLS itself notes that 'automation of routine engineering and design tasks also may reduce the need for workers in this occupation' — but the physical testing, inspection, and hands-on manufacturing support components create a natural barrier. The flat employment outlook reflects AI's already-emerging impact on the more routine digital portions of the job.

title: Mechanical engineers
rationale: Mechanical engineers do substantial computer-based work — CAD design, simulation, analysis, and documentation — where AI tools are already providing meaningful productivity gains. However, a significant portion of the job involves physical prototyping, hands-on troubleshooting, site visits, and overseeing manufacturing processes that require real-world judgment and presence. AI can accelerate design iteration, run simulations, and assist with analysis, but the creative problem-solving, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and physical validation aspects create meaningful barriers to full automation.

title: Medical and health services managers
rationale: Medical and health services managers do substantial knowledge work — budgeting, compliance, scheduling, reporting, and data analysis — that AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, the role also requires significant interpersonal leadership (hiring, motivating staff, representing the facility on boards), physical presence in healthcare settings, and navigating complex human and regulatory relationships that resist full automation. AI will likely make these managers more productive and may reduce headcount growth, but the combination of leadership, stakeholder management, and on-site operational oversight creates a meaningful floor on human involvement.

title: Medical assistants
rationale: Medical assistants split their time between hands-on clinical tasks (taking vital signs, drawing blood, assisting with examinations, giving injections) and administrative/data-entry work (scheduling, billing, entering patient records). AI can meaningfully automate or assist with the administrative side — scheduling, coding, documentation, and record entry — but the physical, patient-facing clinical duties require human presence and cannot be easily automated. The strong projected job growth (12%) reflects that the physical and interpersonal core of the role provides a natural buffer against AI displacement, even as AI tools improve efficiency on the administrative side.

title: Medical dosimetrists
rationale: Medical dosimetrists do highly specialized knowledge work — reviewing scans, calculating radiation doses, and designing treatment plans — that is predominantly computer-based and involves complex data analysis, making it susceptible to AI augmentation. AI is already being developed for automated treatment planning and dose optimization in radiation oncology, which could significantly boost individual productivity or reduce the number of dosimetrists needed. However, the work requires deep clinical judgment, collaboration with a multidisciplinary oncology team, regulatory accountability, and occasional direct patient interaction, which tempers the exposure somewhat.

title: Medical equipment repairers
rationale: Medical equipment repairers perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — installing, diagnosing, and repairing complex electromechanical and electronic medical devices in healthcare settings. The core job requires physical dexterity, on-site presence, and tactile troubleshooting that AI cannot replicate. AI might assist with diagnostics, documentation, or accessing technical manuals, but the actual repair work remains firmly in the physical domain with strong growth projections suggesting limited displacement risk.

title: Medical records specialists
rationale: Medical records specialists work almost entirely on computers, performing data entry, coding, and record management tasks that are highly amenable to AI automation. The BLS itself acknowledges that AI-powered solutions are already making medical coding more efficient, which could dampen demand growth. While some interpersonal coordination with clinicians and regulatory/compliance judgment is required, the core tasks of classifying diagnoses, entering data into EHR systems, and maintaining records are exactly the kind of structured, rule-based digital work where AI excels.

title: Medical scientists
rationale: Medical scientists do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, writing grants and papers, literature review, hypothesis generation — where AI tools are already highly useful and productivity gains are significant. However, a core portion of the job involves hands-on laboratory work, designing and conducting physical experiments, handling biological samples, and overseeing clinical trials, which creates a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement. AI is likely to accelerate research workflows and reduce the number of support staff needed, but the experimental, physical, and regulatory dimensions of the role preserve substantial human involvement.

title: Medical transcriptionists
rationale: Medical transcription is almost entirely a digital, computer-based task — listening to audio recordings and converting them to text — which is precisely what AI speech recognition and natural language processing excel at. The BLS itself notes that technological advances in these areas are already reducing demand and increasing productivity, leading to projected employment decline. The core work product is fully digital with no physical component, and AI can already perform the primary task autonomously, with humans increasingly relegated to editing AI-generated drafts rather than doing original transcription.

title: Meeting, convention, and event planners
rationale: Event planning involves a significant mix of digital knowledge work (budgeting, communications, vendor research, contract drafting) where AI can meaningfully assist, and substantial physical/interpersonal components (site inspections, on-site coordination, relationship-building with clients and vendors, real-time problem-solving during events) that resist automation. AI tools can streamline planning documents, vendor comparisons, and communications, but the core value of the role lies in human judgment, negotiation, and managing complex interpersonal dynamics in physical settings. The job is unlikely to be heavily restructured by AI given its inherently relational and logistical nature, though productivity gains from AI assistance are plausible.

title: Metal and plastic machine workers
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to operate, monitor, and adjust heavy machinery on a factory floor — tasks that cannot be done remotely or purely digitally. While AI and automation (CNC, robotics) are displacing some roles and contributing to the projected 7% employment decline, the core work still requires physical presence, mechanical skill, and real-time intervention in unpredictable manufacturing environments. AI's role is more as a driver of industrial automation (a longer-standing trend) than as a direct cognitive replacement, placing this in the low-to-moderate exposure range.

title: Microbiologists
rationale: Microbiologists split their time between hands-on laboratory work (culturing organisms, running experiments, handling physical specimens) and knowledge-intensive tasks (data analysis, report writing, literature review, grant writing) where AI can provide significant assistance. AI tools are already accelerating genomic analysis, literature synthesis, and experimental design, but the core wet-lab work—isolating cultures, operating specialized equipment, collecting field samples—requires physical presence and manual skill that AI cannot replace. The occupation faces moderate restructuring as AI augments the analytical and writing components but leaves the experimental execution largely intact.

title: Middle school teachers
rationale: Middle school teaching involves significant in-person, physical presence requirements — managing classrooms, supervising students, and building relationships with adolescents — which creates a natural barrier to AI automation. However, AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, grading, assessment design, and personalized learning content, making teachers who use AI substantially more productive. The core human elements of classroom management, mentorship, and social-emotional support for adolescents remain difficult to automate, but the knowledge-work components (curriculum design, grading, parent communication) are increasingly AI-addressable.

title: Military careers
rationale: Military careers span an enormous range of roles — from combat infantry and pilots to healthcare, engineering, and administrative functions — but the defining characteristics of military service (physical fitness requirements, deployment to unpredictable environments, command authority, and national security sensitivity) create strong barriers to AI substitution. AI can assist with intelligence analysis, logistics planning, and administrative tasks, but the core of military service involves physical presence, real-world decision-making under life-or-death conditions, and human leadership that AI cannot replace. Some specialty roles (e.g., intelligence analysts, administrative officers) face higher exposure, but the overall occupation as defined here remains predominantly physical and interpersonal.

title: Mining and geological engineers
rationale: Mining and geological engineers split their time between knowledge work (designing mines, preparing technical reports, analyzing data, planning operations) and physical site work (supervising construction, inspecting mines, monitoring equipment). AI can meaningfully assist with geological analysis, mine design optimization, report writing, and safety monitoring, but substantial portions of the job require on-site presence, physical judgment, and real-world decision-making in unpredictable environments. The combination of complex engineering judgment, regulatory responsibility, and field work creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement, placing this occupation in the moderate-to-high exposure range.

title: Models
rationale: AI image generation and the ability to reuse/digitally alter model images directly threatens demand for human models, particularly for digital advertising, catalog work, and product photography where a physical presence is not strictly required. However, the core of the job involves physical presence — runway shows, trade events, live retail interactions, and posing for physical artists — which AI cannot replicate. The BLS itself notes AI as a factor moderating employment demand, but live and experiential modeling retains inherent human value.

title: Music directors and composers
rationale: Composition work is largely digital and AI is already capable of generating music, writing scores, and arranging pieces, creating significant exposure for composers specifically. However, music directing involves substantial physical presence, real-time human leadership, interpersonal communication, and live performance coordination that AI cannot replicate. The creative and artistic dimensions of both roles, combined with the human relationships and live performance aspects, provide some buffer, but the digital/compositional side of the work faces meaningful AI disruption.

title: Musicians and singers
rationale: Musicians and singers are fundamentally tied to physical performance, live presence, and human artistic expression, which creates a natural barrier to AI automation of the core job. However, AI does pose meaningful indirect threats: AI-generated music can substitute for session musicians and background tracks, AI tools can assist with composition and production, and streaming algorithms already affect how music is discovered and monetized. The live performance and human connection aspects remain resilient, but the recording/studio side of the profession faces real disruption.

title: Natural sciences managers
rationale: Natural sciences managers do substantial knowledge work — drafting reports, reviewing research, budgeting, communicating findings, and setting strategy — all of which AI can meaningfully assist with. However, a significant portion of their role involves physical presence (lab oversight, fieldwork coordination, personnel management, safety compliance) and interpersonal leadership that resists full automation. AI will likely make these managers more productive in their administrative and analytical tasks, but the supervisory, relational, and domain-expert judgment components provide a meaningful buffer against displacement.

title: Network and computer systems administrators
rationale: Network and computer systems administration is predominantly knowledge/digital work — configuring, monitoring, troubleshooting, and securing systems — all of which AI tools can increasingly assist with or automate. The BLS itself projects a 4% employment decline, explicitly citing automation of routine tasks, DevOps encroachment, and Networks-as-a-Service outsourcing. While some physical components exist (installing hardware, cabling), the majority of the job involves information processing, diagnostics, and configuration that AI is rapidly improving at handling.

title: News analysts, reporters, and journalists
rationale: Journalism is fundamentally a knowledge and writing profession — researching, interviewing, analyzing, and producing written or broadcast content — all domains where AI is rapidly advancing. AI can already draft articles, summarize information, generate scripts, and assist with data journalism, meaning fewer journalists are needed to produce the same volume of content. The occupation already faces structural decline, and AI accelerates this by enabling news organizations to automate routine reporting (earnings reports, sports scores, weather) and dramatically boost individual journalist productivity, though some human presence for field reporting and relationship-building provides a partial buffer.

title: Nuclear engineers
rationale: Nuclear engineering is predominantly knowledge work involving complex analysis, design, simulation, and regulatory compliance — all areas where AI can provide meaningful assistance. However, significant portions of the job require physical presence at facilities, hands-on oversight of safety-critical systems, and high-stakes human judgment that regulators and institutions are unlikely to delegate to AI. The highly specialized, safety-critical, and heavily regulated nature of nuclear work creates institutional barriers to AI automation, even as AI tools can accelerate simulation, design optimization, and documentation tasks.

title: Nuclear medicine technologists
rationale: Nuclear medicine technologists perform substantial hands-on physical work — preparing and administering radioactive drugs, operating large imaging equipment, positioning patients, and managing radiation safety protocols — all requiring physical presence in a clinical setting. AI can assist with image analysis and interpretation, record-keeping, and dosage calculations, but the core tasks of patient interaction, radiopharmaceutical preparation, and equipment operation remain human-dependent. The occupation has meaningful knowledge-work components (image quality assessment, protocol selection, data recording) where AI tools are increasingly useful, but the irreducible physical and patient-care elements keep overall exposure moderate.

title: Nuclear technicians
rationale: Nuclear technicians perform substantial hands-on physical work — operating and maintaining equipment, collecting environmental samples, monitoring radiation levels on-site, and responding to contamination incidents — all requiring physical presence in regulated, safety-critical environments. While AI could assist with data analysis, anomaly detection in sensor readings, and documentation, the core job involves physical inspection, equipment calibration, and real-world safety response that cannot be done remotely or digitally. The highly regulated, security-cleared nature of nuclear facilities also creates significant institutional barriers to AI automation of core tasks.

title: Nurse anesthetists, nurse midwives, and nurse practitioners
rationale: APRNs perform highly physical, hands-on clinical work — administering anesthesia, delivering babies, conducting physical exams, and directly treating patients — that fundamentally requires human presence and dexterous skill. AI can assist with documentation, diagnostic support, and treatment planning, but the core of the job involves real-time patient interaction, physical procedures, and clinical judgment in unpredictable medical situations. The strong projected employment growth (35%) further reflects that demand for human practitioners is rising, not being displaced by automation.

title: Nursing assistants and orderlies
rationale: Nursing assistants and orderlies perform almost entirely hands-on, physical care tasks — bathing, repositioning, feeding, and transporting patients — that require direct human presence and physical dexterity. AI cannot bathe a patient, lift someone into a wheelchair, or provide the compassionate human touch central to this role. The only peripheral AI exposure comes from documentation and vital sign recording, which represents a small fraction of the job.

title: Occupational health and safety specialists and technicians
rationale: This occupation has a significant physical, on-site component — inspecting workplaces, testing equipment, demonstrating safety gear, and investigating incidents in factories, construction sites, and other hazardous environments — which limits AI's ability to automate core tasks. However, AI can meaningfully assist with the knowledge-work portions: writing reports, analyzing incident data, identifying trends, researching regulations, and developing training materials. The balance of hands-on fieldwork and regulatory compliance activity places this occupation in the moderate exposure range, where AI augments productivity without fundamentally displacing the need for human presence.

title: Occupational therapists
rationale: Occupational therapy is fundamentally a hands-on, in-person profession requiring physical assessment, direct patient interaction, manual demonstrations, and real-time adaptation to individual client needs in clinical, home, and school settings. AI can assist with documentation, treatment planning suggestions, and administrative tasks, but the core work—physically guiding patients, evaluating functional movement, building therapeutic rapport, and adapting interventions on the fly—requires human presence and judgment. The strong projected job growth (14%) and high physical/interpersonal demands place this occupation at low AI exposure.

title: Occupational therapy assistants and aides
rationale: Occupational therapy assistants and aides perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — guiding clients through therapeutic exercises, lifting and moving patients, setting up equipment, and providing direct human interaction and encouragement. The core of the job requires physical presence and interpersonal compassion that AI cannot replicate. AI might assist with documentation, scheduling, and progress reporting, but these are peripheral tasks; the strong projected employment growth of 18-19% further reflects that this is a human-presence-dependent role unlikely to be displaced by AI.

title: Oil and gas workers
rationale: Oil and gas work is fundamentally physical and hands-on — operating drills, maintaining equipment, working at heights, and performing manual labor in remote or offshore environments. AI and automation may assist with remote monitoring and optimizing drilling operations, but the core tasks require physical presence and mechanical skill that AI cannot replace. The BLS itself notes that robotics and automated drilling technologies may dampen some demand, but the job remains anchored in physical, on-site work with minimal digital knowledge-work components.

title: Operations research analysts
rationale: Operations research analysts work almost entirely in digital domains — collecting data, building quantitative models, running optimizations, and writing reports — all tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. AI tools can already automate significant portions of data collection, statistical modeling, forecasting, and report generation that form the core of this work. While human judgment is still needed for problem framing, stakeholder communication, and translating recommendations into organizational action, the productivity gains from AI are likely to be substantial, meaning fewer analysts may be needed to produce the same output even as demand for the underlying analysis grows.

title: Opticians
rationale: Opticians work primarily in physical, customer-facing settings where core tasks — measuring customers' faces, fitting and adjusting eyewear, helping customers try on frames, and hands-on repairs — require physical presence and manual dexterity. AI can assist with administrative tasks like record-keeping, inventory management, and possibly prescription interpretation, but cannot replace the tactile fitting process or in-person customer service. The growing trend of online eyewear purchasing represents some indirect disruption, but the hands-on dispensing role retains a strong physical barrier to AI automation.

title: Optometrists
rationale: Optometry involves significant physical examination and hands-on patient interaction — using specialized equipment to examine eyes, fitting lenses, and performing minor procedures — which creates a natural barrier to AI automation. However, AI is already making inroads in diagnostic imaging analysis (detecting glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, macular degeneration) and could assist with interpreting test results and generating prescriptions, meaningfully augmenting the knowledge-work portions of the role. The core job still requires licensed clinical judgment, patient communication, and physical presence, keeping overall exposure moderate rather than high.

title: Orthotists and prosthetists
rationale: Orthotists and prosthetists perform highly physical, hands-on work — taking body measurements, fabricating and fitting custom devices, and adjusting mechanical components — that requires direct patient interaction and manual dexterity. AI could assist with documentation, device design optimization, or patient assessment data analysis, but the core tasks of physical measurement, fitting, and fabrication in unpredictable patient-specific contexts are difficult to automate. The strong projected job growth and the irreducibly physical nature of the work limit AI exposure significantly.

title: Painters, construction and maintenance
rationale: Painting is an almost entirely physical, hands-on occupation requiring manual dexterity, physical presence, and real-time judgment about surfaces, materials, and conditions. AI cannot apply paint, prepare surfaces, climb scaffolding, or perform the core physical tasks of this job. While AI might marginally assist with estimating paint quantities or scheduling, these peripheral tasks represent a tiny fraction of the work.

title: Painting and coating workers
rationale: Painting and coating work is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring manual operation of spray equipment, surface preparation, and quality inspection in factory or repair shop environments. While some automation already exists in manufacturing (computer-controlled spray systems), the core tasks involve physical dexterity, sensory judgment (color matching, surface feel), and working in specialized ventilated spaces that AI cannot directly perform.
