AI might marginally assist with process optimization or machine programming, but the physical nature of the work creates a strong barrier to meaningful AI displacement.

title: Paralegals and legal assistants
rationale: Paralegal work is almost entirely knowledge-based and digital — legal research, document drafting, organizing case files, and writing correspondence — all tasks where AI is already demonstrably capable and rapidly improving. The BLS itself explicitly notes that AI is expected to limit employment growth by making paralegals more efficient at research and document preparation, which is reflected in the flat 0% projected job growth. While some interpersonal elements exist (client contact, court appearances), the core work product is fundamentally digital and highly susceptible to AI automation or productivity amplification.

title: Personal financial advisors
rationale: Personal financial advisors do substantial knowledge work — analyzing portfolios, researching investments, and creating financial plans — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, and robo-advisors already handle simpler cases. However, the core value proposition heavily depends on interpersonal trust, relationship-building, and sales skills that are difficult for AI to replicate, and the BLS itself notes robo-advisors will only partially temper demand. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level where AI augments productivity significantly but the human relationship element remains a meaningful barrier to full displacement.

title: Pest control workers
rationale: Pest control work is fundamentally physical and location-dependent, requiring workers to travel to client sites, inspect premises, apply chemicals, set traps, and navigate confined spaces. AI might assist with scheduling, route optimization, record-keeping, or identifying pest species from photos, but these are peripheral to the core job. The hands-on nature of pesticide application, physical inspection, and structural barrier installation cannot be meaningfully automated by AI.

title: Petroleum engineers
rationale: Petroleum engineering involves substantial knowledge work — data analysis, reservoir modeling, well design, and optimization — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, making individual engineers more productive. However, a significant portion of the job requires physical site visits, hands-on troubleshooting at drilling locations, and coordination with field workers in unpredictable environments, which limits full AI displacement. The highly specialized, safety-critical nature of the work and the need for professional judgment in complex subsurface conditions means AI serves more as a powerful tool than a replacement, though productivity gains could reduce headcount over time.

title: Pharmacists
rationale: Pharmacists perform a mix of knowledge-intensive tasks (drug interaction checking, medication counseling, clinical decision support) and physical/interpersonal duties (dispensing medications, administering vaccinations, direct patient care). AI can significantly automate the information-processing aspects—drug interaction screening, prescription verification, inventory management, and clinical decision support—but the patient-facing, regulatory, and hands-on clinical roles create meaningful barriers. The profession is already seeing automation pressure in retail pharmacy (mail-order, automated dispensing), but expanding clinical roles in hospitals and clinics where human judgment and patient relationships are central will partially offset displacement.

title: Pharmacy technicians
rationale: Pharmacy technicians perform a mix of physical tasks (measuring, packaging, labeling medications, operating dispensing equipment, making hospital rounds) and information-processing tasks (data entry, insurance claims, inventory management) that partially overlap with AI capabilities. Automated dispensing systems and AI-driven prescription management are already encroaching on some routine tasks, but the physical handling of medications, direct customer interaction, and regulatory oversight requirements create meaningful barriers. The projected 6% job growth suggests the field is not facing imminent displacement, likely because expanding healthcare demand and pharmacists shifting to patient care roles are offsetting automation pressures.

title: Phlebotomists
rationale: Phlebotomy is fundamentally a hands-on, physical job requiring manual dexterity to draw blood, direct patient interaction, and real-time human presence — none of which AI can replicate. The only digital components (entering sample information into a database, labeling) are minor and peripheral to the core work. AI has essentially no ability to automate the physical act of venipuncture or the interpersonal reassurance of anxious patients.

title: Photographers
rationale: Photography has significant physical and creative components requiring real-world presence, equipment operation, and interpersonal skills with clients, which limits direct AI automation of the core capture work. However, AI is already heavily impacting post-production (automated editing, enhancement, background replacement), stock photography demand (AI-generated images competing with stock photos), and commercial photography (AI image generation reducing need for product shoots). The combination of AI-generated imagery threatening demand, AI-assisted editing reducing post-production labor, and smartphone AI cameras eroding the entry-level market creates substantial indirect exposure, though the physical, relational, and artistic judgment aspects provide meaningful insulation.

title: Physical therapist assistants and aides
rationale: Physical therapist assistants and aides perform highly physical, hands-on work — manual therapy, massage, stretching, patient movement, and exercise guidance — that requires direct human presence and physical dexterity. AI has very limited ability to replicate the tactile, interpersonal, and physical components of this role. While AI might assist with scheduling, documentation, or treatment planning at the supervising PT level, the core daily tasks of PTAs and aides remain firmly in the physical world.

title: Physical therapists
rationale: Physical therapy is fundamentally a hands-on, physical profession requiring direct patient contact, manual therapy techniques, and real-time observation of movement and function that cannot be replicated digitally. AI can assist with administrative tasks, documentation, treatment planning suggestions, and analyzing patient progress data, but the core work — physically manipulating joints, guiding exercises, and providing tactile feedback — requires human presence. The strong projected job growth (11%) reflects that demand is driven by demographic trends rather than being threatened by automation.

title: Physician assistants
rationale: Physician assistants perform substantial knowledge work (reviewing histories, interpreting diagnostics, documentation) where AI can meaningfully assist, but the core of the job requires physical patient examination, hands-on procedures like suturing and setting bones, and real-time clinical judgment in unpredictable medical situations. AI tools may improve efficiency in documentation, differential diagnosis support, and treatment research, but cannot replace the physical presence and procedural skills central to the role. The strong projected job growth (20%) further suggests AI is more likely to augment than displace PAs in the near term.

title: Physicians and surgeons
rationale: Physicians and surgeons have significant physical, hands-on components (surgery, physical exams, procedures) that AI cannot replace, but substantial portions of their work — interpreting diagnostic images, analyzing test results, documentation, treatment planning, and pattern recognition in diagnosis — are highly amenable to AI augmentation. Some specialties like radiology and pathology face higher exposure as their core work is image/data interpretation, while surgeons and emergency physicians face lower exposure due to irreplaceable manual dexterity and real-time physical judgment. AI will likely make physicians more productive and may reduce demand at the margins (especially in diagnostic roles), but regulatory, liability, and patient-trust factors will slow displacement significantly.

title: Physicists and astronomers
rationale: Physicists and astronomers do substantial knowledge work — mathematical modeling, data analysis, writing papers and proposals, developing software — all of which AI can meaningfully assist with, and AI tools like large language models and neural networks are already being used in physics research (e.g., protein folding, particle physics data analysis, gravitational wave detection). However, the core of the job involves deep creative scientific reasoning, designing novel experiments, and operating specialized physical equipment, which remain highly resistant to AI automation. The occupation is likely to see significant productivity gains from AI assistance rather than wholesale displacement, keeping exposure in the high-but-not-extreme range.

title: Plumbers, pipefitters, and steamfitters
rationale: Plumbing, pipefitting, and steamfitting are fundamentally physical trades requiring hands-on work in varied, unpredictable environments — installing pipes, troubleshooting leaks, working in tight spaces, and lifting heavy materials. AI cannot perform these physical tasks and has minimal impact on the core job. AI might marginally assist with cost estimation, blueprint reading, or scheduling, but these peripheral tasks represent a small fraction of the work.

title: Podiatrists
rationale: Podiatry is fundamentally a hands-on medical profession requiring physical examination, surgical procedures, and direct patient care that cannot be replicated by AI. AI may assist with diagnostic image analysis, medical record review, or treatment planning support, but the core work — performing surgery, physical assessments, and patient interaction — requires physical presence and manual dexterity. The occupation has low AI exposure similar to other procedural medical specialties where the physical component dominates.

title: Police and detectives
rationale: Police and detective work is predominantly physical and interpersonal — patrolling, making arrests, responding to emergencies, and interacting with the public in unpredictable real-world environments. AI can assist with peripheral tasks like report writing, database searches, and case analysis, but the core job requires physical presence, human judgment in dynamic situations, and legal authority that cannot be delegated to AI. The occupation's strong institutional, legal, and public-trust requirements further insulate it from significant AI-driven restructuring.

title: Political scientists
rationale: Political science work is predominantly knowledge-based — researching, analyzing data, writing reports, forecasting trends, and synthesizing information from documents and surveys — all tasks where AI is rapidly becoming capable. AI tools can already assist substantially with literature reviews, data analysis, policy evaluation, and drafting reports, making each political scientist significantly more productive and potentially reducing headcount. However, the job retains meaningful human elements such as expert judgment, stakeholder relationships, navigating political nuance, and credibility in presenting findings to government clients, which tempers the exposure somewhat.

title: Postal service workers
rationale: Postal service work is predominantly physical — carrying, sorting, and delivering mail requires human presence and physical stamina — which limits direct AI automation of the core job. However, AI-driven sorting technology, route optimization, and reduced mail volume from digital communications are already displacing workers indirectly, as reflected in the projected employment decline. The sorting/processing sub-role has higher exposure to automation than mail carriers, but the physical delivery component creates a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement.

title: Postsecondary education administrators
rationale: Postsecondary education administrators perform a mix of knowledge work (data analysis, record management, policy development, application review) and interpersonal/relationship-driven work (advising students, building faculty relationships, campus presence). AI can meaningfully automate or assist with data analysis, application screening, scheduling, record-keeping, and communications, but the core of the role involves human judgment, institutional relationships, and physical campus presence. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level where AI tools will enhance productivity and may reduce headcount at the margins, but the relational and institutional leadership dimensions create meaningful barriers to full automation.

title: Postsecondary teachers
rationale: Postsecondary teachers have significant knowledge-work components — course design, grading, research, writing — where AI can meaningfully assist or partially automate, but the core job involves in-person instruction, mentorship, and human relationships that create real barriers to full AI substitution. AI can help with lecture preparation, grading, and research synthesis, potentially making each professor more productive, but the credential-signaling value of human instruction and the interpersonal dynamics of teaching limit displacement. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level: AI will reshape how professors work substantially, but the physical presence requirement and the institutional/social value placed on human educators provide meaningful protection.

title: Power plant operators, distributors, and dispatchers
rationale: Power plant operators work in a hybrid environment — much of their work involves monitoring digital control systems, reading data, and making decisions based on information flows, which AI can increasingly assist with or automate. However, the job also requires physical presence, hands-on equipment checks, emergency response, and operation of complex physical infrastructure in safety-critical environments. The BLS already projects a 10% employment decline driven by automation and smart-grid technology, confirming meaningful AI/automation exposure, but the physical presence requirements, safety-critical judgment, and regulatory licensing constraints (especially for nuclear operators) create significant barriers to full AI displacement.

title: Preschool and childcare center directors
rationale: This role combines significant administrative and knowledge work (budgeting, policy development, curriculum planning, record-keeping) with substantial physical presence requirements — supervising staff in classrooms, meeting with parents and children, and ensuring regulatory compliance on-site. AI can meaningfully assist with the administrative, communication, and documentation tasks, but the core of the job involves interpersonal leadership, relationship-building with families and staff, and physical oversight of a childcare environment that cannot be replicated digitally. The human judgment and trust required in early childhood settings creates a natural ceiling on AI displacement.

title: Preschool teachers
rationale: Preschool teaching is fundamentally a physical, in-person occupation centered on caregiving, emotional support, and hands-on interaction with very young children — tasks that AI cannot meaningfully replicate. While AI might assist with minor administrative tasks like record-keeping or lesson planning, the core job requires constant physical presence, real-time responsiveness to children's emotional and developmental needs, and human relationship-building with both children and parents. The work is inherently embodied and interpersonal, creating a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

title: Private detectives and investigators
rationale: Private investigation is a hybrid occupation combining significant digital/research work (searching records, analyzing online data, writing reports) with substantial physical components (surveillance, field interviews, undercover work) that AI cannot easily replicate. AI tools can dramatically accelerate the digital research and background-check portions of the job, potentially making investigators more productive with fewer needed for routine tasks. However, physical surveillance, in-person interviews, real-time judgment in the field, and the legal/ethical navigation of evidence collection remain human-dependent activities that create a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement.

title: Probation officers and correctional treatment specialists
rationale: This occupation combines significant knowledge work (writing reports, case assessments, rehabilitation planning) with substantial in-person, physical components (home visits, court appearances, drug testing, community supervision) that AI cannot easily replace. AI could assist with report writing, risk assessment tools, and case documentation, but the core of the job — building relationships with challenging clients, conducting field visits, making nuanced human judgments about safety and rehabilitation — requires physical presence and interpersonal skills. The legal and institutional context also creates barriers to AI automation, as decisions carry significant consequences requiring human accountability.

title: Producers and directors
rationale: Producers and directors do substantial knowledge work — script selection, budgeting, scheduling, communication, and post-production oversight — that AI can increasingly assist with, but the core of the job involves real-time human coordination, creative judgment, and on-set physical presence that AI cannot replicate. AI tools can help with scriptwriting, editing, budgeting, and even casting research, making individual producers/directors more productive, but the relational and leadership dimensions (managing talent, negotiating, inspiring creative teams) remain deeply human. The job is a mix of digital knowledge work and physical/interpersonal coordination, placing it in the high-exposure range but not at the top.

title: Project management specialists
rationale: Project management is predominantly knowledge work — planning, scheduling, budgeting, document production, and communication — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly improving and already useful. AI tools can automate status reporting, risk identification, resource scheduling, and document generation, making individual PMs significantly more productive and potentially reducing headcount needed per project. However, the role retains meaningful human elements: building client trust, navigating interpersonal team dynamics, exercising judgment in ambiguous situations, and sometimes being physically present at project sites, which tempers the exposure somewhat.

title: Property appraisers and assessors
rationale: Property appraisal involves a meaningful mix of physical site visits and inspection work alongside substantial knowledge work (data analysis, comparable sales analysis, report writing) that AI can increasingly automate. Automated Valuation Models (AVMs) are already disrupting routine residential appraisals, and AI can assist heavily with data gathering, comparables analysis, and report generation. However, the physical inspection component, local market judgment, regulatory requirements for licensed human sign-off, and the need to defend assessments in hearings provide meaningful barriers to full automation.

title: Property, real estate, and community association managers
rationale: Property management involves a significant mix of physical/interpersonal tasks (inspecting properties, meeting tenants, resolving disputes, showing units) and knowledge work (financial reporting, budgeting, lease administration, compliance). AI can meaningfully automate the administrative and financial components—rent collection tracking, vacancy posting, maintenance request routing, financial reporting—but the core relationship management, physical inspections, and on-site problem resolution require human presence. The BLS itself notes that automation of some tasks may slow employment growth, suggesting moderate but not transformative AI impact.

title: Psychiatric technicians and aides
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — monitoring patients, assisting with daily living activities, restraining violent patients, and providing hands-on therapeutic care in clinical settings. AI cannot replicate the physical presence, human empathy, and real-time behavioral observation required. While AI might assist with documentation or scheduling at the margins, the core work is irreducibly human and physical, and the occupation is actually projected to grow significantly due to rising demand for mental health services.

title: Psychologists
rationale: Psychology involves substantial human interaction, therapeutic relationships, and real-time emotional attunement that AI cannot replicate — the therapeutic alliance itself is a core mechanism of treatment efficacy. However, AI can meaningfully assist with report writing, psychological assessment scoring and interpretation, research analysis, and administrative tasks, making practitioners more productive. The profession's strong licensing requirements, ethical constraints around client confidentiality, and the irreplaceable value of human empathy and judgment in clinical settings create significant barriers to deeper AI displacement.

title: Public relations and fundraising managers
rationale: PR and fundraising managers do substantial knowledge work — writing press releases, crafting messaging, analyzing trends, and planning campaigns — where AI can meaningfully boost productivity. However, the core of the job involves relationship-building, face-to-face donor meetings, managing reputational crises with human judgment, and leading teams, which are inherently human activities that AI cannot replicate. The managerial and interpersonal dimensions create a natural ceiling on AI displacement, placing this occupation in the high-exposure but not very-high-exposure range.

title: Public relations specialists
rationale: Public relations work is predominantly knowledge-based and digital — writing press releases, drafting speeches, managing social media, and crafting messaging — all tasks where AI is already highly capable and improving rapidly. However, the job also requires significant interpersonal skills, relationship-building with media contacts, strategic judgment in sensitive situations, and representing clients in public-facing contexts, which creates meaningful barriers to full automation. AI tools can dramatically boost individual PR specialist productivity (drafting content, monitoring sentiment, analyzing coverage), which may moderate employment growth even as demand for the function persists.

title: Public safety telecommunicators
rationale: Public safety telecommunicators work primarily at computer workstations handling calls, logging information, and coordinating dispatch — all digital tasks where AI is advancing rapidly (voice recognition, automated triage, computer-aided dispatch optimization). However, the job requires real-time human judgment in high-stakes, emotionally volatile situations, empathy with distressed callers, and accountability that creates strong resistance to full automation. AI can meaningfully augment this role (auto-transcription, priority scoring, protocol guidance) but the liability, unpredictability, and human trust requirements make complete displacement unlikely in the near term.

title: Purchasing managers, buyers, and purchasing agents
rationale: Purchasing work is predominantly knowledge-based — analyzing price proposals, evaluating suppliers, maintaining records, and managing contracts — all of which are tasks AI can increasingly automate or significantly accelerate. The BLS itself notes that organizations will likely continue to automate procurement tasks and may use AI, which may limit growth. However, the role retains meaningful human elements such as supplier relationship management, negotiation, site visits, and judgment calls on quality and ethics that provide some buffer against full automation.

title: Quality control inspectors
rationale: Quality control inspection is primarily a physical, hands-on occupation requiring workers to be present on factory floors, operate measuring instruments, and physically handle products — creating a natural barrier to AI automation. However, AI-powered vision inspection systems, machine learning defect detection, and automated measurement tools are already encroaching on core tasks like identifying defects and reviewing output, meaning AI is meaningfully reshaping parts of the job. The flat employment outlook (0% growth) partly reflects ongoing automation pressure, but the physical presence requirement and need to handle unpredictable real-world manufacturing environments keeps this from being a high-exposure role.

title: Radiation therapists
rationale: Radiation therapists perform a mix of technical/clinical tasks and patient-facing work that requires physical presence — positioning patients, operating linear accelerators, and monitoring reactions in real time. AI can assist with treatment planning, tumor localization, and record-keeping, but the hands-on patient care, equipment operation, and interpersonal support for cancer patients remain human-dependent. The slow projected job growth partly reflects efficiency gains from improved radiotherapy techniques, but the physical and clinical nature of the core role limits how much AI can directly displace these workers.

title: Radiologic and MRI technologists
rationale: Radiologic and MRI technologists perform substantial physical work — positioning patients, operating heavy equipment, and providing hands-on patient care — that cannot be automated away. However, AI is increasingly capable of assisting with image analysis and quality assessment, and could reduce the interpretive judgment component of the role. The core physical presence requirement (patient preparation, equipment operation, patient interaction) provides a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement, keeping this in the moderate exposure range.

title: Railroad workers
rationale: Railroad work is fundamentally physical and operational — driving trains, coupling/uncoupling cars, monitoring tracks, and managing rail yards all require physical presence and real-time situational awareness in dynamic environments. AI and automation can assist with scheduling, dispatching, and monitoring systems, and some roles (like locomotive firers) have already been largely automated away, but the core operational tasks still require human operators, especially given safety regulations and certification requirements. The BLS itself notes that automation efforts are limiting employment growth, suggesting modest indirect exposure, but the physical, safety-critical nature of the work creates a natural ceiling on AI displacement.

title: Real estate brokers and sales agents
rationale: Real estate involves significant digital knowledge work — market analysis, document preparation, property listings, client communication — where AI can already provide substantial assistance. However, the core value proposition relies heavily on physical property tours, local relationship networks, trust-building with clients during high-stakes emotional decisions, and in-person negotiation, which creates a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement. AI will likely make individual agents more productive (automated listings, AI-driven market analysis, document generation) but the irreducibly human and physical elements of the transaction process moderate the overall exposure.

title: Receptionists
rationale: Receptionists perform a mix of digital tasks (scheduling, data entry, correspondence, phone handling) that AI can increasingly automate through chatbots, virtual assistants, and automated scheduling systems, and the BLS itself notes employment is constrained by automation. However, a significant portion of the role involves physical presence — greeting visitors, controlling access, escorting guests, and providing in-person customer service — which creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement. The job sits at moderate-to-high exposure: the information-processing tasks are highly automatable, but the physical front-desk presence and interpersonal elements keep it from reaching the very high range.

title: Recreation workers
rationale: Recreation workers primarily perform hands-on, in-person activities — leading sports, arts and crafts, camps, and group programs — that require physical presence and real-time human interaction. AI can assist with minor administrative tasks like scheduling, newsletters, or paperwork, but the core job of motivating, instructing, and supervising participants in physical settings is largely insulated from AI automation. The occupation's reliance on interpersonal skills, physical activity facilitation, and adaptability in unpredictable group environments creates a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

title: Recreational therapists
rationale: Recreational therapy is fundamentally a hands-on, in-person occupation involving direct physical and emotional engagement with patients through activities like sports, aquatics, arts, and community outings. While AI could assist with documentation, treatment plan drafting, or progress tracking, the core therapeutic work requires physical presence, human empathy, and real-time interpersonal interaction that AI cannot replicate. The high injury rate and physical demands further underscore the embodied nature of this role.

title: Registered nurses
rationale: Registered nursing is fundamentally a hands-on, physically present occupation involving direct patient care, administering treatments, operating equipment, and providing emotional support — all of which require human presence and cannot be automated. However, AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, diagnostic support, care plan optimization, and information processing tasks that constitute a meaningful portion of nursing work. The strong projected job growth and irreplaceable physical/interpersonal core of the role limit overall AI exposure, though AI tools will increasingly augment nursing productivity in the knowledge-work dimensions of the job.

title: Rehabilitation counselors
rationale: Rehabilitation counseling is fundamentally an interpersonal, relationship-driven profession requiring empathy, active listening, and in-person client interaction, which creates a natural barrier to AI automation. However, AI can meaningfully assist with administrative tasks like maintaining client records, researching resources, developing rehabilitation plans, and identifying referral services. The core counseling relationship, advocacy work, and nuanced human judgment about client needs and progress remain difficult for AI to replicate, keeping this in the moderate exposure range.

title: Respiratory therapists
rationale: Respiratory therapists perform highly physical, hands-on clinical work — administering treatments, managing ventilators, performing chest physiotherapy, drawing blood, and responding to emergencies — that requires direct patient contact and real-time judgment in unpredictable medical settings. AI can assist with monitoring data, interpreting diagnostic results, or documentation, but cannot replace the physical interventions and bedside care that constitute the core of this role. The strong projected job growth (12%) further reflects that demand for human presence in this occupation is rising, not declining.

title: Retail sales workers
rationale: Retail sales workers perform a mix of physical presence tasks (greeting customers, demonstrating products, stocking shelves) and information-processing tasks (answering questions, processing payments, recommending products) that AI can increasingly handle through chatbots, recommendation engines, and self-checkout systems. The ongoing shift to e-commerce already reflects AI-driven displacement, and AI-powered virtual assistants can replicate much of the informational advisory role. However, the physical store environment, hands-on product demonstrations, and interpersonal relationship-building with customers create a meaningful barrier that keeps this from being fully automatable in the near term.

title: Roofers
rationale: Roofing is an almost entirely physical, hands-on occupation requiring workers to be present on job sites, climbing, lifting, cutting, and installing materials in variable outdoor conditions. AI has essentially no ability to automate the core tasks of measuring, removing, and installing roofing materials. Minor peripheral tasks like estimating material quantities or scheduling could see some AI assistance, but these represent a negligible fraction of the work.

title: Sales engineers
rationale: Sales engineers combine technical knowledge with relationship-building and persuasion, meaning AI can significantly assist with proposal generation, technical documentation, market research, and product configuration analysis, but the core value lies in human trust-building and complex negotiation with clients. The role requires substantial in-person interaction, travel, and reading customer dynamics in real time, which limits full AI substitution. AI tools will make individual sales engineers more productive (better demos, faster RFP responses, smarter lead targeting) but the interpersonal and consultative dimensions create a meaningful barrier to wholesale displacement.

title: Sales managers
rationale: Sales managers do substantial knowledge work — analyzing data, forecasting, budgeting, developing strategies, and communicating — all areas where AI tools are rapidly improving and can meaningfully boost productivity. However, the core of the role involves leading and motivating people, building relationships with customers and partners, and exercising judgment in complex interpersonal situations, which remain difficult for AI to replicate. AI will likely reduce the analytical and administrative burden significantly, but the human leadership and relationship dimensions provide a meaningful buffer against full displacement.

title: School and career counselors and advisors
rationale: School and career counselors do significant knowledge work (analyzing data, writing reports, researching programs) that AI can assist with, but the core of their job is deeply interpersonal — building trust with students, providing emotional support, and navigating sensitive personal situations that require human empathy and presence. AI can augment tasks like résumé review, career matching, and information gathering, but the therapeutic and relational dimensions of counseling create a meaningful barrier to full automation. The occupation sits at a moderate exposure level where AI becomes a useful tool rather than a replacement.

title: Secretaries and administrative assistants
rationale: Administrative assistant work is predominantly digital — document preparation, scheduling, email management, database maintenance, and communication — all tasks where AI tools are already highly capable. The BLS itself notes that AI systems and digital tools are enabling staff to handle tasks previously done by secretaries, contributing to flat/declining employment projections. While some interpersonal elements (greeting visitors, phone calls, coordinating with executives) provide modest barriers, the core work product is fundamentally digital and highly susceptible to AI automation and productivity amplification.

title: Securities, commodities, and financial services sales agents
rationale: This occupation is predominantly knowledge-based and digital — analyzing financial data, communicating with clients, executing trades, and preparing reports — all areas where AI is rapidly advancing. AI has already automated much of routine trading and is increasingly capable of financial analysis, market monitoring, and client advisory functions. However, the high-value relationship-driven aspects (client trust, complex deal negotiation, institutional sales) and regulatory/licensing requirements provide meaningful barriers, keeping this from the highest exposure tier.

title: Security guards and gambling surveillance officers
rationale: Security guards primarily perform physical, on-site duties — patrolling premises, controlling access, responding to emergencies, and deterring crime — that require human presence and real-time judgment in unpredictable environments. AI-integrated surveillance cameras and monitoring systems can automate some observation tasks (particularly for gambling surveillance officers), but the core job still requires humans to physically respond, de-escalate confrontations, and make situational decisions. The BLS itself notes that AI in remote monitoring may limit some employment growth, but the physical and interpersonal nature of the work creates a natural ceiling on AI displacement.

title: Semiconductor processing technicians
rationale: Semiconductor processing technicians work primarily in physical cleanroom environments, operating and monitoring specialized manufacturing equipment, handling silicon wafers, and performing hands-on quality control tasks that require physical presence and dexterity. While AI can assist with process monitoring, data analysis, and defect detection, the core job involves physical manipulation of equipment and materials in a highly controlled environment that AI cannot replace. Some peripheral tasks like reviewing process data and documentation have modest AI exposure, but the fundamentally physical and equipment-intensive nature of the work limits overall AI impact.

title: Set and exhibit designers
rationale: Set and exhibit designers do substantial computer-based work including CAD design, drafting, budgeting, and visual planning, all areas where AI tools are rapidly improving and can meaningfully augment productivity. However, the job also requires significant physical presence (overseeing installations, traveling with shows), hands-on collaboration with directors and curators, and creative spatial judgment that remains difficult to fully automate. AI can accelerate the design iteration and visualization phases but cannot replace the on-site coordination, physical problem-solving, and interpersonal collaboration that are central to the role.

title: Sheet metal workers
rationale: Sheet metal work is fundamentally a physical, hands-on trade requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and on-site presence to fabricate, install, and maintain metal products. While some fabrication tasks are already automated via CNC machinery and CADD systems, the core installation and maintenance work requires human workers in unpredictable physical environments. AI may assist with design, planning, or equipment programming at the margins, but cannot replace the physical labor of bending, cutting, welding, climbing, and fitting sheet metal in construction settings.

title: Skincare specialists
rationale: Skincare specialists perform hands-on physical treatments — facials, hair removal, massages, peels — that require direct human touch and in-person client interaction, creating a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI might assist peripherally with product recommendations, scheduling, or skin analysis tools, but cannot replicate the manual dexterity and physical presence central to the job. The occupation's projected growth further reflects its resilience as a service requiring human contact.

title: Small engine mechanics
rationale: Small engine mechanics perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — disassembling engines, replacing parts, using hand and pneumatic tools — that requires physical presence and manual dexterity in ways AI cannot replicate. AI could assist with diagnostics, repair documentation, or parts lookup, but these are peripheral to the core job. The work is fundamentally tactile and location-dependent, placing it firmly in the low-exposure category.

title: Social and community service managers
rationale: This role combines significant knowledge work (data analysis, grant writing, program evaluation, budget management) with substantial interpersonal and community-facing responsibilities that require human presence and judgment. AI can meaningfully assist with writing proposals, analyzing program effectiveness data, and administrative tasks, but core functions like supervising staff, community outreach, stakeholder relationship management, and navigating complex human service situations resist automation. The mix of office-based knowledge work and direct human interaction places this occupation in the moderate exposure range.

title: Social and human service assistants
rationale: This role is heavily centered on in-person human interaction, relationship-building, and navigating complex social situations with vulnerable populations — tasks that require empathy, trust, and physical presence that AI cannot replicate. While AI could assist with administrative tasks like paperwork, researching available benefits, or coordinating services, the core value of the job lies in interpersonal skills and community-based case management. The occupation's projected growth and its dependence on human judgment in sensitive, unpredictable situations further limit AI's ability to meaningfully displace these workers.

title: Social workers
rationale: Social work is fundamentally built on human relationships, empathy, and in-person interaction with vulnerable populations — qualities that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with administrative tasks like case documentation, resource referrals, and record-keeping, and could potentially support assessment and triage functions. The core therapeutic, advocacy, and crisis-response work requires physical presence, emotional attunement, and trust-based relationships that create a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

title: Sociologists
rationale: Sociologists do predominantly knowledge work — designing research, analyzing data, writing reports, and developing policy recommendations — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and can provide substantial productivity gains. AI tools can already assist significantly with literature reviews, statistical analysis, survey design, and report writing, meaning fewer sociologists may be needed to produce the same output. However, the job retains meaningful human elements such as conducting interviews, building rapport with research subjects, exercising nuanced judgment about social phenomena, and collaborating with policymakers, which provide some buffer against full automation.

title: Software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers
rationale: Software development is almost entirely digital knowledge work — writing code, designing systems, analyzing requirements, and testing — all domains where AI is advancing rapidly and already providing substantial productivity gains through tools like GitHub Copilot, Claude, and GPT-4. AI can automate significant portions of coding, test generation, debugging, and documentation, meaning fewer developers may be needed to produce the same output, and QA/testing roles face particular disruption as automated AI-driven testing matures. The score is 8 rather than 9-10 because the job still requires substantial human judgment in architecture decisions, stakeholder communication, creative problem-solving, and navigating complex organizational contexts that AI cannot yet fully replicate.

title: Solar photovoltaic installers
rationale: Solar PV installation is fundamentally a physical, hands-on trade requiring workers to climb roofs, handle heavy equipment, cut and assemble structural components, and perform electrical connections on-site — tasks AI cannot perform. AI might assist with system design, configuration planning, or scheduling at the periphery, but the core work is irreducibly physical and location-dependent. The occupation's strong projected growth further reflects that demand is driven by physical installation needs that cannot be digitized.

title: Special education teachers
rationale: Special education teaching is fundamentally a human-presence job requiring physical interaction, emotional attunement, and individualized relationship-building with students who have disabilities — core aspects that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with administrative tasks like drafting IEPs, lesson planning, progress tracking, and adapting curriculum materials, which represent a significant portion of the workload. The job's irreducible need for in-person instruction, behavioral management, and trust-based relationships with vulnerable students creates a strong natural barrier to deeper AI displacement.

title: Special effects artists and animators
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely computer-based and digital, with core tasks involving creating graphics, animation, and visual effects using software — all domains where AI is advancing rapidly with tools like generative AI image/video synthesis, AI-assisted animation, and procedural generation. The BLS itself notes that AI may dampen demand by automating routine animation and special effects tasks. While high-end creative judgment, artistic vision, and client collaboration still require human skill, the trajectory of AI in this field is steep and the slow projected job growth (2%) likely understates disruption as AI tools make individual artists dramatically more productive.

title: Speech-language pathologists
rationale: Speech-language pathology is fundamentally a hands-on clinical profession requiring direct patient interaction, physical assessment of swallowing and articulation, and therapeutic relationship-building that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, treatment planning, diagnostic support, and some teletherapy applications. The strong projected job growth (15%) reflects genuine human demand that AI is unlikely to displace, as the core therapeutic work requires physical presence, nuanced listening, and compassionate human engagement.

title: Stationary engineers and boiler operators
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to operate, inspect, maintain, and repair boilers and mechanical equipment in person. While AI and automation can assist with monitoring gauges and logging data, the core tasks — physical inspection, manual repairs, troubleshooting equipment failures, and operating valves and controls — require physical presence and dexterity that AI cannot replicate. The slow projected employment growth reflects gradual automation of monitoring functions, but the irreducibly physical nature of the work keeps AI exposure very low.

title: Substance abuse, behavioral disorder, and mental health counselors
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally built on human therapeutic relationships, empathy, and real-time interpersonal interaction, which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI can assist with documentation, treatment plan drafting, and administrative tasks, but the core counseling work — building trust, active listening, crisis intervention, and guiding behavioral change — requires human presence and connection that clients specifically seek. The strong projected job growth (17%) reflects sustained demand for human counselors, though AI tools may modestly improve productivity in documentation and resource referral tasks.

title: Surgical assistants and technologists
rationale: Surgical assistants and technologists perform highly physical, hands-on work in operating rooms — passing instruments, maintaining sterile fields, positioning patients, and directly assisting surgeons during procedures. These tasks require physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time responsiveness in unpredictable environments that AI cannot replicate. While robotic surgical systems exist, they augment surgeons rather than replace the supporting roles of technologists, and the physical, safety-critical nature of the work creates a strong barrier to AI displacement.

title: Survey researchers
rationale: Survey research is predominantly knowledge work done on a computer — designing surveys, analyzing data with statistical software, interpreting results, and writing reports — all tasks where AI is rapidly capable. The BLS already projects employment decline driven by big data and data mining replacing traditional survey methods, a trend AI will accelerate further. While some human judgment is needed for survey design and client communication, the core analytical and methodological tasks are highly automatable, placing this occupation firmly in the very high exposure range.

title: Surveying and mapping technicians
rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork (visiting sites, operating instruments, navigating terrain) and digital/knowledge work (GIS data processing, CAD software, map creation). AI and automation (drones, improved sensors) are already streamlining data collection, and AI tools can increasingly assist with image processing, map updating, and data integration. However, the significant physical presence required for fieldwork, equipment operation, and site verification creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement, keeping exposure moderate rather than high.

title: Surveyors
rationale: Surveying is a hybrid occupation with significant fieldwork requiring physical presence, travel to remote locations, and hands-on measurement in unpredictable terrain, which limits AI's direct substitution. However, the office-based components — processing GPS/GIS data, preparing maps and legal documents, analyzing records, and using CAD software — are increasingly automatable, and drone/remote sensing technologies are already noted as limiting employment growth. AI can substantially augment productivity in data interpretation and mapping tasks, but the licensed, legally accountable, and physically present nature of boundary certification creates a meaningful barrier to full displacement.

title: Tax examiners and collectors, and revenue agents
rationale: Tax examination and revenue work is predominantly knowledge-based — reviewing financial documents, analyzing tax returns, interpreting regulations, and making determinations — all of which are tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. AI can already automate much of the routine return review, anomaly detection, and compliance checking that forms the core of this work. However, the job retains meaningful human elements such as negotiating with taxpayers, conducting field audits, exercising judgment in complex cases, and navigating sensitive interpersonal situations with delinquent taxpayers, which moderates the score somewhat.

title: Taxi drivers, shuttle drivers, and chauffeurs
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical — driving a vehicle through real-world environments — which creates a natural barrier to AI automation in the near term. However, autonomous vehicle technology (Waymo, Tesla, etc.) represents a long-term existential threat that could eventually displace most of these workers, making the indirect AI exposure significant. The BLS still projects strong growth through 2034, suggesting the transition is not imminent, but the trajectory of self-driving technology means this occupation faces more AI-driven disruption than most physical jobs.

title: Teacher assistants
rationale: Teacher assistants perform predominantly in-person, hands-on work including supervising students, providing physical assistance to special needs students, and building interpersonal relationships — all of which require physical presence and human connection. While AI could assist with some peripheral tasks like recordkeeping or grading, the core duties of one-on-one student support, behavioral guidance, and physical care are highly resistant to automation. The occupation's projected slight decline likely reflects budget pressures more than AI displacement, and the irreplaceable human element in working with children and students with disabilities keeps AI exposure low.

title: Technical writers
rationale: Technical writing is almost entirely digital knowledge work — researching, drafting, editing, and structuring text-based documentation — which sits squarely in AI's strongest domain. LLMs are already capable of generating first drafts of manuals, how-to guides, and FAQs, and can rapidly synthesize technical specifications into plain language. The BLS itself notes that AI tools are slowing employment growth by boosting individual productivity, and the projected 1% growth over a decade reflects this displacement pressure, with the occupation's small size making even modest AI-driven efficiency gains highly impactful.

title: Telecommunications technicians
rationale: Telecommunications technicians perform predominantly physical, hands-on work — climbing towers, splicing fiber optic cables, installing equipment in homes and central offices — which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI can assist with remote diagnostics, network monitoring, and troubleshooting guidance, but the core job requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and on-site problem-solving. The occupation is already projected to decline slightly due to technological improvements in infrastructure durability rather than AI displacement specifically.

title: Tellers
rationale: Bank teller work is already being heavily disrupted by digital banking, ATMs, and automation — the BLS projects a 13% employment decline over the next decade. The core tasks (processing transactions, verifying accounts, handling cash) are increasingly handled by ATMs, mobile banking apps, and AI-driven systems, while the remaining human role shifts toward complex customer service interactions. The job has a physical/interpersonal component that provides some barrier, but the transaction-processing and information-handling aspects are highly automatable, and AI-enhanced kiosks and virtual tellers are already reducing headcount per branch.

title: Top executives
rationale: Top executives perform substantial knowledge work — analyzing reports, formulating strategy, and communicating decisions — that AI can increasingly assist with, pushing exposure upward. However, the core value of executive roles lies in human judgment, relationship-building, accountability, negotiation, and leadership in unpredictable organizational contexts, which AI cannot replicate. AI will likely make executives more productive by automating analysis and drafting tasks, but the interpersonal, political, and trust-based dimensions of leadership create a meaningful ceiling on displacement.

title: Tour and travel guides
rationale: Tour and travel guides work is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — leading groups through locations, adapting to real-time situations, and building rapport with clients in person. While AI could assist with research, itinerary planning, and logistics, the core value of the job lies in live human presence, storytelling, and dynamic interaction that AI cannot replicate in the field. Some peripheral tasks like reservation management or content research may be streamlined by AI, but the occupation's growth trajectory and inherently embodied nature limit overall exposure.

title: Training and development managers
rationale: Training and development managers do predominantly knowledge work — designing curricula, assessing needs, managing budgets, and communicating with stakeholders — all of which AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, the role involves substantial human elements: managing staff, building relationships with department leaders, facilitating live training, and exercising organizational judgment that requires contextual understanding of company culture. AI can accelerate content creation, needs analysis, and program evaluation, but the interpersonal leadership and organizational navigation components provide a meaningful buffer against full automation.

title: Training and development specialists
rationale: Training and development specialists do substantial knowledge work — designing curricula, creating learning materials, assessing needs, and evaluating programs — all areas where AI can meaningfully assist or partially automate. However, a significant portion of the role involves live facilitation, interpersonal coaching, and collaborative delivery that requires human presence and relationship-building. AI can accelerate content creation and needs analysis but is unlikely to fully replace the instructional and human-connection aspects of the job in the near term.

title: Transportation, storage, and distribution managers
rationale: This role combines significant knowledge work (budgeting, planning, compliance monitoring, supply chain optimization) with substantial physical presence requirements (warehouse oversight, staff management, facility inspections) and real-time operational decision-making. AI can meaningfully assist with route optimization, inventory forecasting, compliance tracking, and logistics analytics, but the core managerial functions—supervising workers, resolving on-the-ground disruptions, negotiating with suppliers, and leading teams—still require human judgment and physical presence. The growing complexity of e-commerce supply chains may actually increase demand for these managers even as AI tools enhance their productivity.

title: Travel agents
rationale: Travel agents work primarily on computers and phones, researching destinations, booking reservations, and communicating with clients — all tasks where AI is already highly capable. AI-powered travel planning tools, chatbots, and booking platforms can automate much of the information-gathering, itinerary-building, and reservation-making that defines this role. However, the occupation retains some resilience through personalized client relationships, handling complex or emergency situations, and the value of firsthand destination experience that clients specifically seek out from human agents.

title: Tutors
rationale: Tutoring involves significant human interaction, relationship-building, and adaptive instruction that AI cannot fully replicate, particularly for students with disabilities or those needing emotional support and motivation. However, the BLS itself notes that generative AI will reduce demand by automating many core tutoring tasks like explaining concepts, providing practice problems, and giving feedback — all fundamentally digital knowledge-transfer activities. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level because while the interpersonal and adaptive elements provide some buffer, the core academic content delivery is highly susceptible to AI substitution.

title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and real-time, requiring officials to be present at sporting events, make split-second judgments based on direct observation, and manage interpersonal disputes with athletes and coaches. While AI and video replay technology can assist with some calls (e.g., hawk-eye systems, goal-line technology), the core job requires physical presence, situational awareness, and human authority that AI cannot replace. AI may incrementally assist with replay review but is unlikely to displace the need for on-field human officials in the foreseeable future.

title: Urban and regional planners
rationale: Urban and regional planners do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, GIS mapping, report writing, feasibility assessments — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, pushing exposure upward. However, a significant portion of the job involves stakeholder engagement, public meetings, political negotiation, and site visits that require human presence and interpersonal judgment. AI tools can meaningfully accelerate the analytical and documentation tasks, but the community-facing, regulatory, and relationship-management dimensions create a natural ceiling on full automation.

title: Veterinarians
rationale: Veterinarians perform substantial physical, hands-on work including surgery, wound treatment, physical examinations, and operating medical equipment that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with diagnostic support, medical record analysis, treatment planning, and interpreting imaging results, making the knowledge-work portions of the job more efficient. The core of the occupation remains grounded in physical presence, manual dexterity, and direct animal care, creating a natural ceiling on AI's ability to reshape the role fundamentally.

title: Veterinary assistants and laboratory animal caretakers
rationale: This occupation is overwhelmingly physical and hands-on — feeding, bathing, restraining, and caring for animals in person — with essentially no core tasks that can be digitized or automated by AI. AI might marginally assist with scheduling, record-keeping, or monitoring systems, but these are peripheral to the job's primary duties. The high injury rate and physical demands underscore how deeply rooted this work is in real-world, unpredictable animal interactions.

title: Veterinary technologists and technicians
rationale: Veterinary technologists and technicians perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — restraining animals, administering anesthesia, collecting samples, providing nursing care — that requires physical presence and manual dexterity in unpredictable environments. AI may assist with diagnostic image analysis or record-keeping on the periphery, but the core job involves direct animal handling and real-time clinical judgment that cannot be automated. The occupation's high injury rate and physical demands underscore its fundamentally non-digital nature.

title: Waiters and waitresses
rationale: Waiting tables is fundamentally a physical, in-person job requiring carrying food, reading customer moods, and providing hospitality — tasks AI cannot directly perform. Some peripheral tasks like order-taking are being automated via kiosks and self-service technology, which explains the slight projected employment decline, but the core human interaction and physical service elements remain resistant to AI replacement. AI has minimal impact on the daily work of actually serving customers in a dining environment.

title: Water and wastewater treatment plant and system operators
rationale: This occupation involves significant physical presence, hands-on equipment operation, chemical handling, and on-site inspections that cannot be done remotely or digitally. However, AI and automation are already impacting the monitoring and control aspects — computerized systems increasingly manage treatment processes, contributing to the projected 7% employment decline. The core job still requires skilled workers physically present to respond to emergencies, maintain equipment, and operate complex systems, limiting AI exposure to moderate levels.

title: Water transportation workers
rationale: Water transportation work is fundamentally physical and place-bound, requiring workers to be aboard vessels operating in dynamic, unpredictable maritime environments. While AI could assist with navigation planning, route optimization, and some monitoring tasks, the core duties — operating vessels, maintaining machinery, managing cargo and passengers in real-time, and responding to emergencies at sea — require physical presence and hands-on skill that AI cannot replace. Autonomous shipping technology exists in early stages but faces enormous regulatory, safety, and technical barriers, keeping human crews essential for the foreseeable future.

title: Web developers and digital designers
rationale: Web development and digital design are almost entirely computer-based occupations where core tasks — writing code, designing interfaces, creating graphics, and testing applications — are precisely the domains where AI is advancing most rapidly. AI tools like GitHub Copilot, generative design platforms, and no-code/low-code builders are already substantially augmenting or replacing routine coding and design tasks, meaning fewer workers can produce the same output. While client communication, creative judgment, and complex problem-solving provide some buffer, the fundamentally digital nature of the work and the steep AI capability trajectory in coding and design place this occupation firmly in the very high exposure range.

title: Welders, cutters, solderers, and brazers
rationale: Welding is fundamentally a hands-on, physical trade requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and real-time judgment in variable environments — qualities that AI cannot replicate directly. While robotic welding automation exists in manufacturing, this affects the broader industry rather than representing AI reshaping the cognitive or knowledge work of skilled welders. AI might assist peripherally with blueprint interpretation or quality inspection, but the core job remains physical and resistant to AI displacement.

title: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives
rationale: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives blend significant interpersonal, relationship-driven work (client visits, negotiations, trust-building) with digital tasks like order processing, reporting, and communication that AI can readily assist with or automate. AI tools such as CRM automation, chatbots, and lead-generation platforms are already encroaching on inside sales functions, while outside sales roles retain a human advantage in complex negotiations and relationship management. The BLS itself notes that AI and automation may limit employment growth, but the physical travel, face-to-face selling, and nuanced client relationship aspects provide a meaningful buffer against full AI displacement.

title: Wind turbine technicians
rationale: Wind turbine technicians perform predominantly physical, hands-on work — climbing 200-foot towers, replacing mechanical components, lubricating parts, and rappelling to service blades — all of which require physical presence and cannot be done remotely by AI. AI might assist with remote monitoring, diagnostics, and maintenance scheduling, but the core job requires a human body in a physically demanding and often hazardous environment. The digital/analytical components (using computers to diagnose electrical malfunctions, collecting turbine data) are a minor fraction of the overall role.

title: Woodworkers
rationale: Woodworking is fundamentally a physical, hands-on occupation requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and direct interaction with materials and machinery. While CNC machines and computer-aided design already automate some aspects of the work, the core tasks — cutting, shaping, assembling, finishing, and installing wood products — require physical presence and skilled manual labor that AI cannot directly replace. AI may marginally assist with design interpretation or machine programming, but the occupation's primary barriers to automation are physical, not informational.

title: Writers and authors
rationale: Writing and authorship is almost entirely a digital, knowledge-based occupation where the core work product — text — is precisely what modern AI systems excel at generating, editing, and refining. AI tools like large language models can already draft articles, blog posts, ad copy, scripts, and other content at scale, directly threatening the volume of work available to human writers. While originality, deep subject expertise, unique voice, and human relationships with editors/clients provide some buffer, the fundamental task of producing written content is highly automatable, and many lower-tier writing jobs (copywriting, content writing, blogging) are already being displaced.

title: Zoologists and wildlife biologists
rationale: Zoologists and wildlife biologists split their time between physical fieldwork (collecting specimens, observing animals in remote habitats, handling wildlife) and knowledge work (data analysis, writing reports, GIS modeling, conservation planning). AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and writing components — species identification from images, population modeling, literature synthesis — but the core fieldwork requiring physical presence in unpredictable natural environments creates a substantial barrier to AI displacement. The occupation's heavy reliance on outdoor skills, direct animal observation, and hands-on data collection keeps overall AI exposure moderate.
